Entertainment.title: Nottingham maternity scandal to be dramatised in new series
Entertainment.text:
The largest maternity scandal in NHS history is set to become the focus of a new television drama starring Harry Potter actor Jason Isaacs.

Last month, a review found more than 500 mothers and babies suffered potentially avoidable harm or died due to failings at Nottingham University Hospitals (NUH) NHS Trust.

Jack and Sarah Hawkins - whose story will be told in the new TV series - campaigned for years for answers, after their daughter Harriet died before she was delivered at Nottingham City Hospital, which is run by the trust, in 2016.

NUH said the review was a "watershed moment" and it was "committed to creating a better maternity service" for women, babies and families in its care.

The programme will be based on the ITV documentary Maternity: Broken Trust, which followed families affected by the trust's failings.

Speaking to the BBC about learning the scandal would become a television drama, Sarah said: "I thought, that's crazy, it's so exciting.

"And I think the reason it's so exciting, is because for so long Nottingham families just felt so alone and that we were the issue.

"And I feel like now, we've come to the point where people are believing us."

The Hawkins were one of the first families to raise the alarm about maternity services at the trust.

When a hospital review found "no obvious fault", and stated Harriet had died of an infection, Jack and Sarah - who had both worked for the trust - pushed for an external review.

That external review, published in 2018, found 13 failings and concluded Harriet's death was "almost certainly preventable".

The wider review into maternity failings in Nottingham began in September 2022, and was led by senior midwife Donna Ockenden.

Sarah said: "The Ockenden review has demonstrated there there has been preventable harm and preventable death.

"And we did the amazing documentary before but I do feel like there is an element where people disconnect if it's a documentary because its just so horrific, people don't want to feel like there is preventable harm or death.

"With the drama, people will be able to get into it and there is another opportunity for people to see what Nottingham families have been through for so long."

Jack said he and Sarah knew there was a problem at the trust after Harriet died.

"We knew from the behaviour of the hospital in 2016 - and it's not the hospital, not the bricks and mortar - but the people," he said.

"And this is one of the interesting things about drama, you will get to see the behaviours of people that we were up against, who wanted us to pretend that it was one of those things, that it was normal, that it was reasonable that Harriet died, and that we were not surrounded by so many others.

"How can the avoidable death or harm to one child or mother be OK, and how can it be 500?

"We've normalised it, but this drama will show you the bones of what, actually, a fight against the NHS looks like, and the reality of what that fight was."

Jack will be played by Jason Isaacs in the drama, which is being produced by Cuba Pictures and directed by Jim Loach.

Laura Warner, who directed Maternity: Broken Trust - which won a Royal Television Society journalist award - is also an executive producer of the new series.

She said despite following the Hawkins for a year for the documentary, there was "only so much" they could fit into it.

"What you can't fit into a documentary is just the sheer depth of trauma and pain and frustration and struggle, that not just Jack and Sarah, but all the families in Nottingham have been through," she told the BBC.

"We barely scratched the surface with the documentary, as much as we tried hard, but I feel that this way, dramatising it, we can really try and put ourselves more into Jack and Sarah and the other families' shoes."

A transmission date for the series has not yet been announced.

Tracy Pilcher, chief nurse at NUH said: "Jack and Sarah Hawkins have been through unimaginable pain since the tragic loss of their daughter, Harriet, while in our care.

"Not only did we fail to care for them in our hospitals, but we also repeatedly failed them when they tried to get answers, and for that I am truly sorry.

"Through the pain and grief, they have campaigned relentlessly for answers and for change.

"The Independent Maternity Review into our services was a watershed moment for them and all other families involved in the review, getting the answers that they have fought so hard for.

"But the work doesn't stop there, there is still much to do to improve maternity care, and we are absolutely committed to listening, learning and creating a better maternity service for the women, babies and families in our care."

Last month, a review found more than 500 mothers and babies suffered potentially avoidable harm or died due to failings at Nottingham University Hospitals (NUH) NHS Trust. Jack...

Entertainment.title: Ofcom opens investigation into Married at First Sight UK
Entertainment.text:
Married at First Sight UK is facing renewed pressure after the broadcast regulator launched an official investigation into the hit Channel 4 reality TV show.

In a statement on Monday, Ofcom said it was investigating "a fairness and privacy complaint" about the prime-time show.

In May, a BBC Panorama investigation revealed rape allegations from two female cast members on the show - allegations the men involved deny.

An Ofcom fairness or privacy complaint can be lodged by individuals who participated in TV or radio programmes. Complaints like these usually relate to claims of being treated unfairly or having unwarranted infringements of privacy on programmes.

It is understood the watchdog is considering a complaint of unjust or unfair treatment from a cast member of Married at First Sight UK.

At least one other cast member previously complained to Ofcom about their treatment on the show, but the watchdog dismissed their complaint as it found the broadcaster had no case to answer.

An Ofcom spokesperson said such complaints are considered "on a case-by-case basis through a detailed and careful assessment process".

Separately, in a letter to MPs in June, Ofcom said it would examine the findings of Channel 4's review into welfare on the show, and would "urgently" consider whether any further action is required.

At the time, the watchdog called the allegations exposed by Panorama "shocking and deeply disturbing".

Earlier this month, BBC News revealed that a cast member from the show had been arrested on suspicion of rape following the Panorama programme.

The individual is not being named; nor is the alleged victim. Anyone who makes an allegation of rape is entitled to anonymity by law.

Billed as a "bold social experiment", Married at First Sight UK - known to many as MAFS - sees single people agree to "marry" total strangers, after meeting for the first time at their mock weddings.

The UK version, which has been running for 10 seasons, is one of Channel 4's biggest shows, with audience figures often topping three million.

In May, Channel 4 pulled all episodes of the show from its streaming service just hours ahead of the transmission of the Panorama investigation.

No decision has yet been made on whether to broadcast the latest series of the show, which has already been filmed.

The broadcaster has commissioned a review into contributor welfare on the show, which is due to be finished later this summer. However, it only intends to publish "a summary of findings", rather than the full report.

Lawyers for CPL, the independent production company that makes the UK version of the show, have previously said its welfare system is "gold standard" and industry-leading.

If you have more information about this story, you can reach Noor by email at noor.nanji@bbc.co.uk

In a statement on Monday, Ofcom said it was investigating "a fairness and privacy complaint" about the prime-time show. In May, a BBC Panorama investigation...

Entertainment.title: Tributes paid to influential soul singer Ray King
Entertainment.text:
Tributes have been paid to Ray King, one of the most influential singers in the ska and 2 Tone music genres, following the announcement of his death.

Born Vibert Cornwall in St Vincent and the Grenadines, he came to the UK in 1961 and arrived in Northampton, before moving to Coventry two years later, after being told it was the place to become a singer.

Pete Chambers, the founder of the Coventry Music Museum, said King had a stage presence "like James Brown".

"He was the kind of guy who controlled the stage with just one look, one little movement of the arm or whatever. He was a real professional and such a lovely guy and an influential guy."

In an interview with the Herbert Art Gallery and Museum, external in 2010, King said his musical influences as a young man and performer ranged from Sam Cooke, Nat King Cole, and Elvis Presley to Pat Boone and Doris Day.

He formed the Ray King Soul Band and performed throughout Coventry, with their first gig at The Walsgrave Club in the city.

The reputation of the band's professionalism and popularity led to a six-month residency at the Playboy Club in London, with the likes of Jimmy Hendrix and Paul McCartney joining the band on stage on an ad-hoc basis.

Entertainment.title: ‘The Kardashian era is over’: TikTok’s ‘povvo king’ takes on the billionaires
Entertainment.text:
It was ice cubes that tipped Shabaz Ali over the edge. “A woman online spent her entire day making content about ‘designer ice cubes’,” he recalls. “She’d freeze flavoured waters or fruit juices and show the whole process in tedious detail. I was like, ‘There’s no way she’s got a proper job or has ever struggled for money. Only someone wealthy would have the time to do this.’ It vexed me, so on a whim, I made a reaction video saying ‘On today’s episode of I’m Rich, You’re Poor …’ Soon my TikToks were getting 30m views and thousands of comments. It blew my mind.”

Ali, AKA @ShabazSays, has since become a cult celebrity for his sarcastic commentary on how the other half live. His scathing takedowns of the “rich and ridiculous” have won him more than 4 million followers. Now he’s transferring his viral fame to our TV screens.

It all began when former chemistry teacher Ali, 32, took to his bedroom in Blackburn during lockdown and amused himself by mocking the smugly privileged. In his crosshairs were those who humblebrag about their perfect lives and woo-woo habits. Tongue firmly in cheek, he styled himself as “King of the Povvos” and leader of the “#PovvoGang”.

“It was reclaiming an insult,” he says. “I grew up working class and heard ‘povvo’ so much. The community we’ve built is warm, honest and self-deprecating. They’re saying, ‘We don’t have much but at least we’re here’. Povvo is a badge of honour now.”

The disconnect between influencers and their audience is increasingly jarring. “They post about bespoke Bentleys and private jets. Who are you making this for? [US socialite] Becca Bloom really bothers me. Millions of people watch her cats eating out of £20,000 bowls. She has a £100k Louis Vuitton trunk full of Hermès bags. Yet her followers genuinely think they’re friends.”

In Ali’s corner of the internet, a backlash was brewing: “People were tagging me in dozens of videos per day, pointing out tone-deaf displays of wealth.” He calls it “a movement” that is helping to move the dial on unrealistic standards. “There’s been a massive shift in the last few years,” says Ali. “The Kardashian era is over. We don’t want airbrushed reality any more. We want it raw, authentic and unfiltered.”

In these days of doomscrolling, aspirational figures fill a void. “As a teacher, I saw how social media warped teenagers’ perception of the world. Wealth influencers, trad wives and Andrew Tate provide something that’s missing from their lives. The illusion of working hard and getting your reward has been shattered. They’re seeing their parents and teachers struggle. Young people dream of opulence but can’t have it. They’d say, ‘I don’t need to study, I’m going into business. Richard Branson didn’t do his GCSEs.’ I was like, ‘He’s one person!’ There are 3,400 billionaires in a global population of 8.3 billion. That’s a lot of disappointed people.”

Amid the cost-of-living crisis, his videos provided a reality check. “I was telling my students, ‘This lavish stuff online isn’t right. It’s not real’. That’s why I started taking the mickey. It snowballed.”

His BBC documentary, Shabaz Asks: Are Billionaires Bad?, finds Ali investigating extreme wealth and its real-world impact. With his trademark playful wit, he asks whether the mega-rich are a symptom of a broken culture or if they can be a force for good.

Wealth hoarding gives him “the ick”. “What annoys me most is that the gap is widening,” he says. “We’re getting more billionaires, who are finding more tax loopholes and ways to hoard assets. I genuinely think 90% of them don’t care. You can’t be a billionaire and be a good person.”

In a bid to understand more, he attempts to meet a billionaire in person. Ali approaches the Kardashians, Beckhams, Trumps, Elon Musk, Jeff Bezos and Beyoncé for an interview. “Kim K definitely saw my message. She didn’t reply, though. A couple of people hearted it. That was it.”

Instead he meets a range of people who are both pro and anti-billionaire. These include the Green party’s Zack Polanski. “He’s the only person on the party political spectrum who’s speaking out about billionaires,” says Ali. “Everybody else is too busy trying to protect their donors. Many of his arguments resonated. I’m a lifelong Labour voter but might I vote Green now? Possibly.”

He spends time with Apprentice winner turned “eight-figure entrepreneur” Joseph Valente (“fascinating character but he uses a lot of hairspray”). He flies to the Colorado ski resort of Vail to stay with Phones 4U founder John Caudwell. “I hate billionaires but I liked John, which was hard,” admits Ali. “I just think he’s unaware of his own power and privilege. He told me, ‘You can’t whisper in the ears of governments. I had a meeting with Keir Starmer and he didn’t take my advice.’ The fact that he even said that sentence! You got into that room because of money. The prime minister might not have taken your advice but there’s someone whose advice he did take. I admire John’s philanthropy but fundamentally we still disagree. Your rich friends are still wankers, mate!”

Ali takes the opportunity to poke around inside Caudwell’s kitchen, hoping to find one of the vast “curated” fridges he satirises online. “I genuinely thought I was going to get the internet lifestyle with everything neat and labelled but it was like a normal person’s fridge. It was dirtier than mine!” Did making the film challenge Ali’s views? “It didn’t alter my beliefs, it cemented them. I still think we don’t need billionaires and they should be doing more for the rest of us.”

He’s told that if we make billionaires pay more tax, they will leave the UK and take their wealth with him. “Let them go!” he says. “Let’s look over the hill and see if the grass is greener. It’s just scare tactics. They ain’t going anywhere. They’ve benefited too much from this country and have too much of a stake here to suddenly leave. Tax them, I say. Tax diamonds. Tax yachts.” He laughs. “If they don’t pay up, move them all to the Isle of Wight and turn it into a giant prison for rich people.”

This future star’s debut documentary ends with a shameless appeal for a full series. “We’ve already started talking about the next film,” he says. “Skincare, cosmetics and plastic surgery is the route I’m keen to go down. Shabaz asks: can I turn back time? That’s the question I want to pose. Watch this space.”

In the meantime, he believes society is approaching tipping point. “I think we’re on the brink of something big,” says Ali with a twinkle of zeal in his eyes. “People are so sick of wealth inequality. We’re French Revolution era, ready to overthrow Marie Antoinette.”

Entertainment.title: ‘His chords are like sheets of diamond ice’: the beguiling psych-folk of late cult songwriter Ed Askew
Entertainment.text:
In a grainy television clip from 1986, a flame-haired and neckerchiefed troubadour plucks away at a lute-like instrument, harmonica under chin, the gentle ripples giving way to a voice full of longing. This performance, uploaded to YouTube in 2010, has generated only a handful of comments, but peruse any of them (“Ed the Great!”; “pure gold”) and you begin to understand the passion that Ed Askew – an under-the-radar US poet, artist and musician – has inspired over the years.

“He was living in a world that was for people who didn’t fit in,” Askew’s collaborator, Jerry David DeCicca, says on the free-spiritedness of his friend, whose musical life began with discovering the 10-stringed tiple as a teenager in the 1950s and ended with his death a year ago, aged 84, on the verge of him releasing his album The Final Painting, now being released posthumously this month.

Its producer, DeCicca, first fell for what he calls Askew’s “singularity” in the early 00s. But in recent years, he has captured the hearts of many more of music’s most characterful contemporary acts: from Sharon Van Etten and Bill Callahan, both of whom lend their vocals to The Final Painting, to Glasgow psych-folk band Trembling Bells who brought Askew to London’s Cafe Oto in 2017, and indie-Americana singer-songwriter Steve Gunn, who accompanied him at gigs in the early 2010s. “I got to tour with him in 2011 and felt his Jesus-y vibes,” Callahan says. “Jesus with a little scamp mixed in.” For Callahan, there is a “primordial” element in Askew’s music, that “makes it feel like the world is evolving right before your eyes when his song is playing. There’s a holy aspect to it – those piano chords constructed like sheets of diamond ice.”

From the scent of a marigold to the death of a Kennedy, what stands out the most about Askew’s songwriting is his flâneur-like observations, evoking the quotidian and the tremendous. In 2011, Askew cited his early influences as Kurt Weill, John Cage and Erik Satie, while others have tried to compare Askew to the likes of Robert Wyatt, Gaslight-era Bob Dylan or the Incredible String Band. The truth is that his baroque songwriting bears more resemblance to the multiple-angled paintings of Cézanne – an artist he greatly admired – or the writing of Joe Brainard mixed with the poetry of Lorca, than it does any of its musical equivalents.

Askew’s song Moon in the Mind appeared on the soundtrack of Netflix’s smash-hit series Baby Reindeer in 2024, an inclusion that was welcome and unexpected in the last year of his life, DeCicca says. But for the most part, Askew wasn’t concerned with acclaim or commerciality. “He just wasn’t wired that way,” DeCicca says; instead, Askew simply focused on writing his fine-spun songs on a day-to-day basis.

He displayed that beatnik essence from the very beginning. Born in Connecticut in 1940, Askew studied painting at Yale School of Art in the early 1960s, before moving to New York for a brief spell, playing for tips in the “basket house” folk music venues of the East Village in 1967. It was here that he attracted the attention of New York-based record company ESP-Disk – usually more inclined towards free jazz giants such as Sun Ra and Pharoah Sanders – who put out his 1968 self-titled debut (later retitled Ask the Unicorn), but soon shelved his follow-up Little Eyes, which wasn’t released until 2002.

Over the course of his life, Askew put out approximately 50 albums between labels, archival releases, live albums and self-releases, “with more still unreleased in his vault”, says DeCicca. This was mostly a home-recorded and self-published enterprise funded by house painting and art teaching, plus welfare assistance for a time – before he returned to the studio in 2011. Regarding a song on the new record titled Someone, with Callahan on vocals, DeCicca wonders if Askew is waiting in a social security office while they’re calling out numbers: “16”, “22” and “46” are delivered by Callahan with his usual deadpan delivery. “I think if most people wrote a song like that it would be about their agitation but Ed’s point of view is so different,” DeCicca says. But who knows if it’s really about social security at all, such is the impressionistic nature of Askew’s lyric writing.

Jay Pluck, Askew’s close friend and collaborator, points out just how pioneering Askew’s psych-folk songwriting was in the 1960s. “It was quite brave for him to be writing openly gay love songs so early on,” he tells me. And yet: “Over the years, I noticed that no one really knew that he was gay and I don’t know why – there are gay love songs on Ask the Unicorn and Little Eyes.” Pluck mentions a later song called Lamar, recorded on cassette tape in the late-90s, in which Askew is “professing his love in a Greek paean kind of way”.

Would Askew have described himself as a gay songwriter, I wonder? “I think he would,” Pluck answers – before pausing. “Although he could be a contrarian. If you asked him if he thought he was a great gay artist, he’d say no. But if you left off ‘gay’ he’d be like, why?” Pluck laughs. For Askew, his sexuality was simply who he was, as much a part of his songs as anything else.

Yet for Askew, “like most obscure musicians, there was never that Searching for Sugar Man blast,” DeCicca says, citing the 2012 documentary that launched Mexican-American musician Rodríguez out of obscurity and into late-in-life fame. “What allowed Ed to gradually find a global audience was not one event shining a spotlight; it was that he lived, day in and day out, as a true artist, sharing meaningful breadcrumbs for more and more people to discover.”

It’s a slow-burn tale in the age of the algorithm, and a fitting one for a musician who lived life on his own terms: “I play music for you to feel,” he once sang.

“There is no trying in his music,” Callahan says. “It just is.”

Entertainment.title: The Odyssey: jaw-dropping action, dark magic and dolphin cameos – discuss with spoilers
Entertainment.text:
The Odyssey looks set to be the blockbuster hit of the summer, with global opening weekend grosses of $264m, critical rave reviews including five stars from the Guardian’s Peter Bradshaw, and reports of fans traversing continents to experience Christopher Nolan’s epic tale of gods and monsters in one of the few dozen screens worldwide with the capability to screen Imax in 70mm. It also has the potential to rule the box office for the coming weeks, with few other true blockbusters to be released this summer, with the exception of Spider-Man: Brand New Day (hence the cast’s humiliation ritual of a press tour). Read on for a spoiler-packed breakdown of the epic battles, narrative twists and out-of-nowhere cameos that bring richness to Nolan’s idiosyncratic vision of ancient life.

A monumental spectacle

After Oppenheimer won best picture at the 2024 Oscars, Nolan knew he had a once-in-a-lifetime chance to realize a decades-long dream of bringing Homer to the big screen. “OK, I’ve now got an opportunity to make a film I wouldn’t be able to make otherwise,” he recently told the Guardian. You can see every penny of The Odyssey’s reported $250m budget on screen. The landscapes of Ithaca are breathtakingly beautiful, and there’s a creepy pall to our hero’s journey to the underworld that Nolan achieved by shooting on the black sands of Iceland’s beaches under the hazy light of the midnight sun. (Fun fact: Nolan also used Icelandic glaciers as the backdrop for the alien planet of Interstellar.)

Nolan filmed in six countries to achieve the range of landscapes in the film, which also included Scotland, Greece, Italy and Malta, and, more controversially, Western Sahara. Controlled by Morocco, the region is often referred to as Africa’s last colony, with the UN classifying it as a non-decolonized territory. “Audiences coming to see The Odyssey deserve better,” the artist and writer Mohamed Sleiman Labat wrote in an essay for the Guardian. “Those cinematic shots sold to them as the places and moments where historical epics took place were captured at the expense of the Sahrawi people’s suffering.”

The film’s epic scenes in the Mediterranean were filmed in the ocean, with Nolan eschewing CGI in favor of real roiling seas and Odysseus and his crew aboard the only functional Viking ship in the world (the delightfully named Draken Harald Hårfagre). A replica of the boat was built for scenes shot in a colossal water tank on the Universal lot. “There were these two jet engines, like 737 engines, blowing water on us,” Damon has said. “It was definitely about as hard as you can make water work in a controlled environment.”

Technical wizardry

Nolan has been a champion of Imax for two decades: in 2008, The Dark Knight was the first major Hollywood film to use the cameras for its action sequences. The camera set-up has been used increasingly prominently in his films since then. Nolan used half a dozen Imax cameras to shoot the film, all enclosed in soundproof “blimp” casing to mask their loud operational whirr.

The team also worked with an LED company to create lighting rigs they called “pyrohedrons” (fire pyramids) to give the illusion that warm firelight was casting a glow over interior and night-time scenes. “I wanted the camera to see what the human eye would see,” Nolan told the New York Times.

Bringing the divine down to earth

In Homer’s epic poem, gods are “self-interested beings, whose interventions in human lives are motivated primarily by their own desires, whims, and preferences”, as classicist Emily Wilson put it in her translation. Yet Nolan’s vision of how gods, demi-gods and mortals interact is somewhat earthier than in Homer, with Zendaya’s Athena less of a fearsome divine force than a pal and a confidante to Odysseus.

Charlize Theron is always a treat (even in her ropey Netflix fare), but Nolan’s interpretation of nymph Calypso may not feel strong enough for some viewers to work as a framing device for the bulk of the film. In Homer, the nymph is a cruel enslaver who holds Odysseus captive on her island; Nolan sees the character as lonely and somewhat pitiable. It’s worth wondering what Theron would have done with a spikier version of the role – she excels as a cartoon villain.

Like classicist Emily Hauser, who recently wrote for the Guardian about Nolan’s omissions, some viewers may also miss the Homeric princess Nausicaa. While interacting with Odysseus, she sniffily pooh-poohs him as “an unpromising man” – her barbs would have added a little light relief to the picture.

When Lupita Nyong’o was announced as Helen of Troy – the face that launched a thousand ships – rightwing commentators denounced the casting choice for predictably small-minded reasons. It is ironic that the character is afforded little screen time: when Tom Holland’s Telemachus goes to visit Helen and her husband, Menelaus (Jon Bernthal) in Sparta midway through the film, the famed beauty is near mute, with a grisly scar covering the left side of her face. It’s a bold choice from Nolan, but a little odd that we never find out the cause of her injury.

Odysseus’s loyal wife Penelope (Anne Hathaway) is a steely and cunning presence despite the suitors’ invasion of her palace. “We wanted to make sure that she did not look like she was mourning her husband,” said costume designer Ellen Mirojnick, who dressed Hathaway in the red and blue colors of Ithaca. “She was always viewed as the queen.” The actor has likened her character to a slowly simmering volcano that eventually erupts in scenes where she curses the suitors and pushes her double-crossing servant Melantho (Mia Goth) to certain death while the palace is at war.

One of the film’s most memorable scenes sees Odysseus and his men face the sirens, whose alluring song can lure sailors to their death. As their boat approaches their island, Odysseus is tethered to a mast to hear their beautiful yet terrible song while it is muffled to the audience’s ears, as if we, like Odysseus’s first mate Eurylochus (Himesh Patel), have our ears plugged with wax. The sound is “all the things you want it to be, then all the things you wish you never wished for”, says Odysseus, as Nolan powerfully leaves it to the audience’s imagination to fill in the blank.

Some of the film’s most touching moments come from John Leguizamo, barely recognisable with white hair and a dirty smock in the role of Eumaeus, the nearly-blind swineherd that remains loyal to Odysseus to the very end. As Greek military leader Agamemnon, Benny Safdie strikes a fearsome presence in a smart bit of against-type casting from Nolan. Meanwhile, not all cameos were planned: a pod of dolphins who made a surprise appearance on a day of shooting ended up making the film’s final cut.

Yet perhaps most surprising of all is the casting of rapper Travis Scott as a bard storyteller who opens the movie with a story of Odysseus’s heroic exploits at Troy. As he orates, the character pounds his staff on the floor in the tempo of dactylic hexameter (which as every good classicist knows, is the original meter of Homer’s poem). “I cast him because I wanted to nod towards the idea that this story has been handed down as oral poetry, which is analogous to rap,” Nolan said in an interview with Time.

It’s likely far too early to make many accurate predictions about next year’s Oscars, given that many of the awardsiest-seeming films won’t premiere until fall film festivals. But Nolan is one of the Academy’s favorite directors, winning seven out of Oppenheimer’s 13 nominations in 2024 and a respectable three out of eight for Dunkirk a few years prior. The Odyssey seems like a lock for nominations in best picture, best director and most of the craft categories, leaving the acting categories as a toss-up.

There’s no debate about who the male lead of the film is, but there’s all to play for in the supporting categories, with Holland and Robert Pattinson eligible for supporting actor and Zendaya, Nyong’o and Theron potential competitors in the supporting actress category – and Hathaway, if Universal decides not to run her in lead. But I’d personally lean towards Leguizamo for his turn as Eumaeus, and Samantha Morton’s staggering, scene-stealing role as Circe, a witch who turns Odysseus’s men into pigs.

Being something of an outsider to the Hollywood star system hasn’t stopped Morton getting two Oscar nominations to date (for Sweet and Lowdown and In America). With the weight of Nolan’s film behind her, I’ll be gunning for her to go all the way this time.

Examining the true cost of war

There’s a good case to be made that The Odyssey’s best sequence is its final 30 minutes, where Odysseus finally makes it back home with the help of Athena and sneaks into his Ithaca palace disguised as a beggar man. After Penelope challenges the men to string his bow – and Odysseus is the only one able to – he throws off his rags and reveals his true identity.

His face-off with the suitors isn’t the merciless slaughter that Homer described – it’s a fair fight with both sides armed – but the suitors, led by Pattinson’s slimy Antinous, are no match for the master of the house. His recapturing of Ithaca is bittersweet, interspersed with flashbacks to the “fires of anarchy and pain” that blazed through Troy as the Greeks sacked the city. There’s no glory in war for Nolan, echoing the sentiments of Dunkirk and Oppenheimer. As Odysseus puts it: “We have broken the fragile bond between men.”
Entertainment.description: The Odyssey looks set to be the blockbuster hit of the summer, with global opening weekend grosses of $264m, critical rave reviews including five stars from the Guardian’s Peter Bradshaw, and reports of fans traversing continents to experience Christopher Nolan’s epic tale of gods and monsters in one of the few...
Entertainment.author: SkunkNet Wire - theguardian.com

BreakingNewsTab.title: Burnham pulls back on suggestion to increase tax-free personal allowance
BreakingNewsTab.text:
Andy Burnham has pulled back from suggestions about increasing the tax-free personal allowance.

The £12,570 threshold has been frozen since 2021, resulting in more people being dragged into paying tax as earnings increase.

Burnham told the Times over the weekend that the "frustration about the personal allowance" he had heard while campaigning in the Makerfield by-election was "lodged in my mind".

The prime minister then told reporters on Monday that "all of this will be looked at" in the next budget but conceded any change would be expensive and "difficult given the financial circumstances in which we find ourselves".

Those close to Burnham have since told BBC News that any changes to the personal allowance are not part of his initial plan to tackle the cost of living.

It comes after Burnham told his first cabinet meeting: "We've got to show fiscal discipline. We've got to show that our commitment to the fiscal rules is real and we're prepared to make difficult decisions in relation to that."

Burnham has committed to Labour's 2024 manifesto, which included a promise not to increase the basic, higher, or additional rates of income tax.

That would rule out increasing the top rate of income tax paid by higher earners to offset the cost of lifting the personal allowance.

The Institute for Fiscal Studies has estimated that unfreezing the threshold could end up costing £8.5-9bn a year.

Burnham said on Monday that he was "just kind of showing I have visibility of the issue and the impact it's had."

He told reporters it would be "looked at" in the Budget, but added: "Obviously it's difficult, given the financial circumstances in which we find ourselves."

Government sources told the Telegraph on Tuesday that there were "no plans" to announce any changes to the £12,570 personal allowance "in the short term".

The £12,570 threshold has been frozen since 2021, resulting in more people being dragged into paying tax as earnings increase. Burnham told the Times over the weekend that the "frustration about the personal allowance"...

BreakingNewsTab.title: Ann Widdecombe was hit on head 21 times with hammer, prosecutors say
BreakingNewsTab.text:
Ann Widdecombe was hit on the head with a hammer 21 times at her home as she ate lunch in an attack that was caught on camera, prosecutors have told Westminster Magistrates' Court.

Prosecutor Kashif Malik said the man charged with her murder, Joshua Kerry, arrived at her house on 8 July in a red Vauxhall Corsa and entered through the front door.

Kerry, who prosecutors say was in the property for two minutes and was wearing black gloves, then allegedly hit Widdecombe repeatedly with a hammer in her kitchen, tipped her from her chair and took her wallet before driving off.

The 28-year-old from South Yorkshire later also appeared at the Old Bailey and was remanded in custody.

At the Old Bailey hearing, prosecutor Alistair Richardson said the attack on Widdecombe had been carried out with a "ferocity of violence".

Malik told the court that Widdecombe did not attend a pre-arranged online interview with Channel 5's Matt Allwright show that had been scheduled for just before 13:00 BST on 8 July.

The show's production team tried to contact Widdecombe through her personal assistant, who then asked Widdecombe's gardener to check on her, the court heard.

Prosecutors said the gardener entered the house via the open front door on 9 July and found Widdecombe lying face down on her kitchen floor with a serious head injury.

Malik told the chief magistrate that security camera footage from her home in Haytor, Devon, showed she had been fatally attacked the day before.

He added that police were still investigating whether there was a terrorism connection or political motivation for the attack.

Kerry, from Rotherham, was charged with one count of murder on Monday night following an investigation led by counter-terrorism detectives.

He appeared emotionless in the dock and spoke only to confirm his name, date of birth and address during the short hearing at Westminster Magistrates' Court. He was not asked to indicate a plea.

Later on Tuesday, he also appeared at the Old Bailey. It is expected that he will be remanded at HMP Belmarsh in south-east London.

He will next appear at the same court on 9 October for a case management hearing.

A provisional trial date has been listed for 8 June 2027.

The court heard that a pathologist had given a provisional cause of Widdecombe's death as a blunt force injury to the head.

An earlier inquest hearing, that was opened and adjourned on Tuesday, heard that emergency services were called on the day Widdecombe was found, but she was pronounced dead at 12:20.

The hearing was also told that while a post-mortem examination had taken place, "at this stage the precise medical cause of death has yet to be established".

Widdecombe's career in politics spanned decades.

She served as Conservative MP for Maidstone in Kent for 23 years and was appointed to ministerial roles in Sir John Major's government in the 1990s.

Following her departure from the Commons, Widdecombe appeared on the BBC's Strictly Come Dancing in 2010 and was a runner-up on Celebrity Big Brother eight years later.

She later represented South West England as a Member of the European Parliament (MEP) for the Brexit Party and in 2023 she joined Reform UK as a party spokeswoman.
BreakingNewsTab.description: Ann Widdecombe was hit on the head with a hammer 21 times at her home as she ate lunch in an attack that was caught on camera, prosecutors have told Westminster Magistrates' Court. Prosecutor Kashif Malik said the man charged with her murder, Joshua Kerry, arrived at her house on 8 July in a red Vauxhall Corsa and...
BreakingNewsTab.author: SkunkNet Wire - bbc.co.uk

BreakingNewsTab.title: Iran war has cost US $37.5bn so far, Hegseth says, as Pentagon seeks billions more
BreakingNewsTab.text:
The war with Iran has cost the US some $37.5bn (£28bn) so far - an increase of nearly $8bn since the last estimate in May, Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth has said.

Hegseth told the Senate Appropriations Committee it was urgent that lawmakers approve another $87bn in congressional funding for the Pentagon, with $67bn of that destined for operations in the Middle East.

The hearing was disrupted several times by anti-war protesters and ended in a shouting match between the defence secretary and a senator.

It was Hegseth's first appearance on Capitol Hill since hostilities between the US and Iran restarted last week and comes after three American soldiers were confirmed killed in the region.

The White House asked Congress in April for $1.5tn for the Pentagon over the next fiscal year, which would raise American military spending to an all-time high in the modern era.

The US carried out an 11th consecutive day of strikes on Tuesday. Iran has been retaliating with strikes of its own against the US and its allies in the Middle East.

The defence secretary began Tuesday's testimony by saying, "we live in a dangerous world" and that it required bold and swift action.

After being interrupted by protesters, Hegseth went on to say his funding request came at an urgent time.

"Without these funds, we face critical shortfalls that threaten our ability to also simply pay our service members, rapidly replenish equipment and munitions, and sustain vital operations without disruption," Hegseth said.

Every dollar appropriated, he added, would be used to build the "lethality" of US military forces and "reinforce peace through strength".

Senate Appropriations Committee chairwoman Susan Collins, a Republican, questioned Hegseth over what the impact would be if the budget needs were not met.

Hegseth said current and future training for military members would need to be cut without the funds, as he repeatedly blamed former President Joe Biden's administration for what he said was an underfunded military.

The most senior Democrat on the committee, Patty Murray, pushed back on the funding request, saying it "does not make a lot of sense".

Asked by Democratic Senator Dick Durbin if he had an estimate for how much the war in Iran has cost so far, Hegseth gave the $37.5bn figure.

Two Democratic sources and a third source familiar with the matter told the BBC's media partner CBS that they believe the actual cost of the Iran war is much higher.

They said the current estimate did not include costs like construction for damaged American bases.

There had been no official government estimate of the US financial cost of the war since 12 May, when a senior Pentagon official put the figure at $29bn.

The Pentagon previously told lawmakers the war had cost the US some $11.3bn in the first week.

The Pentagon chief argued that if approved, the funding request would be a "generational investment" that would make sure the US avoided "playing catch-up" with the militaries of other nations.

Iran and the US agreed a ceasefire in April, but the truce broke down and hostilities resumed last week.

At Tuesday's hearing, New York Democratic Senator Kirsten Gillibrand accused the Trump administration of seeking "unlimited money for bombs" from Congress, while American citizens cannot afford to "feed their families".

She asked why Hegseth was asking for tens of billions of dollars for a war the president said had already been won.

The hearing culminated in a shouting match between Hegseth and Democratic Senator Gary Peters.

Peters called Hegseth a "failure" and said the Trump administration had created another "forever war".

Hegseth told Peters "shame on you", adding that the Michigan senator had "Trump derangement syndrome".

Most Democrats are opposed to the supplemental funding request, and there is discontent in Republican ranks too.

Some of the fiscal hawks in the Republican Party want spending cuts elsewhere to offset the budget.

Either way, the House of Representatives leaves town on Thursday and the two chambers of Congress will not be jointly back in session until September.

Even if Republicans can muster the votes, it's going to be a couple of months before the Pentagon can get the funds.

During Hegseth's hearing, Trump - who was at the White House - posted a list of longer, bloodier US wars, noting their duration and death toll.

He wrote on Truth Social that 18 had died so far in the US war with Iran, which began on 28 February.

On Tuesday, the Pentagon announced the death of Sgt Michael Emmanuel Swinton, a 30-year-old US soldier, in Iraq. It also said another US soldier Sgt Angel Rampersad, 28, was believed dead in Jordan.

A day earlier, the military identified two other US service personnel killed in action in Jordan as Pfc Isabella Gonzales, 19, and 1st Lt Tyler Feehan, 25.

Trump is due to go to an air base in the US state of Delaware on Wednesday morning as the soldiers' flag-draped coffins arrive home.

Hegseth told the Senate Appropriations Committee it was urgent that lawmakers approve another $87bn in congressional funding for the Pentagon, with...

BreakingNewsTab.title: More women in their 20s are getting type 2 diabetes
BreakingNewsTab.text:
England is seeing a "worrying increase" in young women in their 20s developing type 2 diabetes, experts at Imperial College London have warned.

The rise has largely occurred since the Covid pandemic and is closely linked to increasing obesity levels, according to an analysis of NHS data from 2011 to 2024, external.

Type 2 diabetes is serious, and can lead to severe complications, such as heart attacks and strokes.

When it develops at a younger age, the disease tends to be more aggressive, raising the risk of devastating complications and cutting lives short, according to Diabetes UK.

It is vital that those at risk are diagnosed early to prevent long-term organ damage, the charity says.

Type 2 diabetes can sometimes be put into remission by eating a healthy diet and keeping slim without needing to rely on glucose-lowering medications.

The researchers say newer obesity treatments - such as GLP-1 jabs and pills - might help, alongside healthy eating advice.

About 90% of the six million people in the UK living with diabetes have the type 2 form of the disease rather than type 1, or insulin-dependent, diabetes.

Risk increases with age and older adults still make up the largest proportion of new diagnoses.

But 12,000 people under the age of 30 are now living with type 2 diabetes, with women making up more of those younger cases, according to NHS England estimates, external.

The most common symptoms include feeling very tired, peeing more than usual and feeling thirsty all the time.

Researchers at Imperial analysed NHS data from the English National Diabetes Audit, external to study trends over time.

The figures show the disease is decreasing slightly among older adults – who still make up the largest proportion of new diagnoses.

But it is increasing rapidly in people under 40, with some of the sharpest rises in women aged 20 to 29.

At the same time, body mass index (a measure of obesity) at diagnosis has been increasing more quickly among younger adults than in older age groups, hinting at a likely cause.

Obesity rates in England have worsened since the pandemic, with nearly one in three people now affected, according to separate NHS data, external for England.

The largest increases over time have been in younger adults.

Between 2019 and 2025, the number of new obesity cases rose by 16% in people aged 20-29, and almost 20% in those aged 30-39, while rates fell among adults aged 60-79.

"We think that the earlier onset of severe obesity in young people is increasing their type 2 diabetes risk in early life and pulling down their age at diagnosis," lead researcher Dr Shivani Misra says.

"This is really worrying given the poor health outcomes from early-onset type 2 diabetes."

She says much of the discussion about rising rates of the disease among younger people has focused on ethnic minority groups, which have traditionally been considered at higher risk.

But the new data suggests the trend extends well beyond those communities.

"We now see that incidence is also increasing in white populations too, showing that this public health challenge is broadening across the generations," she added.

Diabetes UK says better follow-up care also needs to be offered to mums-to-be diagnosed with gestational diabetes in pregnancy because it increases the risk of type 2 diabetes after childbirth.

Some studies have shown the chance of type 2 diabetes also increases after being ill with Covid, but that evidence is not conclusive.
BreakingNewsTab.description: England is seeing a "worrying increase" in young women in their 20s developing type 2 diabetes, experts at Imperial College London have warned. The rise has largely occurred since the Covid pandemic and is closely linked to increasing obesity levels, according to an analysis of NHS data from 2011 to 2024, external....
BreakingNewsTab.author: SkunkNet Wire - bbc.co.uk

BreakingNewsTab.title: 'I can't afford to turn the oven on': 7.4m households struggling to buy essentials
BreakingNewsTab.text:
Four years into the cost of living crisis, almost two-thirds of low-income families say they struggle to buy essentials such as clothes, heating and food, according to anti-poverty charity the Joseph Rowntree Foundation (JRF).

A record 62% of low-income families were unable to afford an essential item in the past six months, the charity found - the equivalent of 7.4m households, up from 7.1 million a year ago.

Almost half of those surveyed had skipped a meal or cut portion sizes to save money.

Newly appointed Prime Minister Andy Burnham has promised to set out how he will "give people some breathing space, help with the cost of living," including cutting VAT on electricity bills from October.

Elaine Yates, 77, says money worries mean she "can't remember" the last time she used her oven to cook a hot meal.

"I don't look after myself. I can't remember the last time I put the oven on. I have got an air fryer that I probably used about three weeks ago.

"I had two slices of toast yesterday and two Weetabix and fruit is all I've had to eat today."

The pensioner lives by herself in rural Northamptonshire after being widowed five years ago. Following 20 years as a full-time carer for her husband, her finances are "extremely tight", so she has felt particularly vulnerable to price rises since he passed away.

"It's really hard times, not just for me, but for thousands of others. My car costs me an arm and a leg. The fuel prices are rocketing up again, and I live in a village, so I need a car because I've got arthritis and I can't walk very far. I spend about £50 a week on petrol, which is more than my food bill."

Elaine often gives lifts to others in her village who don't have transport, only to find herself unable to shop alongside them.

"I've got one friend who buys clothes and I sit outside the shop because I can't afford to go in," she says. "I can't remember the last time I treated myself."

In the winter, Elaine rarely turns the heating on, and when she does she keeps the thermostat at a low 13C.

Zoe Hayden, 35, is a single parent who fell into debt after being made redundant from her retail job while on maternity leave.

Zoe, who lives in North Oxford, is now debt-free and completing a plumbing apprenticeship. But she says rising costs are still a challenge when feeding a family.

"Over the last five years, it's been absolutely bonkers, the way that things have increased. I remember going into the shop and being like, wow, nearly everything I'm buying has increased by 50p or 75p. That's a lot of money when you're struggling.

"I went into the shop the other day and I spent £45 on basic stuff, not even two bags of shopping. It just means you have to make even more sacrifices. You're not living your best life, you're just about surviving."

She would also like Burnham to go beyond cheaper energy bills and look to fund free or affordable activities for children in low-income families, such as youth clubs, holiday schemes and sports camps.

Separate research from charity Christians Against Poverty found about 35% of adults in the UK worry about their finances every day.

As the VAT announcement will save households about £45 a year, "the fear of juggling budgets that simply don't balance remains", says Juliette Flachm, Acting Head of Policy and Public Affair.

"With incomes failing to keep pace with the high cost of essentials, many of the people we see are left facing impossible decisions."

JRF's chief economist Chris Belfield said: "Andy Burnham starts his time as prime minister at a time when the cost-of-living crisis has never been more widely felt.

"Government policy can work. We need greater intervention to control rental prices, make energy affordable and improve the adequacy of Universal Credit so everyone can afford the essentials."

A government spokesperson said: "We're determined to turn the tide on poverty after years of rising hardship. Our recent statistics show that effort is beginning to make a difference - household incomes have risen 5% in real terms, food bank usage has fallen, and food insecurity is down.

"Work is underway to tackle the cost-of-living pressures through measures such as increasing the National Minimum Wage, removing the two-child limit, implementing the first ever sustained above inflation increase to Universal Credit, and launching the £1bn Crisis and Resilience Fund which will act as a genuine safety net for those in financial crisis."

The figures come from the Joseph Rowntree Foundation's poverty tracker, which surveys about 4,000 people from households in the lowest 40% of incomes.

The charity noted that food inflation averaged 4.1% between May 2025 and May 2026, compared to 3.6% for overall inflation, which has greater impact on lower-income households because they spend more of their income on food.

But it also noted that the number of poorer households that couldn't afford to keep their homes warm has fallen by half a million in two years, after the government reduced average household energy bills by about £150 a year through changes to green levies and support like the warm homes discount.
BreakingNewsTab.description: Four years into the cost of living crisis, almost two-thirds of low-income families say they struggle to buy essentials such as clothes, heating and food, according to anti-poverty charity the Joseph Rowntree Foundation (JRF). A record 62% of low-income families were unable to afford an essential item in the past...

BreakingNewsTab.title: UK humanitarian worker monitored for Ebola in London hospital
BreakingNewsTab.text:
A humanitarian worker in the Democratic Republic of Congo has been medically evacuated to the UK after a potential healthcare‑related exposure to Ebola.

The UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA) said the individual, who is a UK resident and has not been named, has no symptoms, and the move is purely precautionary.

The individual remains well but is being assessed and monitored at a London hospital, the statement from UKHSA said.

The hospital has not been named but the Royal Free in north London has a high level isolation unit and is the primary national centre for high consequence infectious diseases.

The incubation period of Ebola ranges from two to 21 days and the humanitarian worker will be required to isolate throughout.

The individual had been working with a humanitarian organisation supporting the Ebola outbreak response.

Ebola is a highly dangerous pathogen. Unlike flu or Covid, it is not airborne and spreads through direct contact with bodily fluids.

Symptoms come on suddenly and start like the flu or malaria, with fever, headache and tiredness. As the disease progresses, vomiting and diarrhoea develop and it can lead to organ failure. Some, but not all, patients develop internal and external bleeding.

Richard Pebody, director of epidemic and emerging infections at UKHSA, said: "The risk to the general public remains low.

"This individual has been transferred out of an abundance of caution, and we're pleased they remain well."

There are currently no confirmed cases of Ebola in the UK, the agency said.

The UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA) said the individual, who is a UK resident and has not been named, has no symptoms, and the move is purely precautionary. The...

BreakingNewsTab.title: Judge rules Trump must hand over financial records sought by BBC in defamation case
BreakingNewsTab.text:
A judge in Florida has ruled President Donald Trump must hand over financial records sought by the BBC as it defends itself in his $10bn (£7.5bn) defamation lawsuit.

The order came during the first hearing in the case, in which the US president is suing the corporation over an episode of Panorama that edited together different sections of his speech on the day of the US Capitol riot on 6 January 2021.

Judge Enjoliqué Lett granted a motion by the BBC's legal team to compel the release of financial records held by the Donald J Trump Revocable Trust, which he created to manage his businesses while he is president.

The judge's ruling at Tuesday's hearing in Miami can still be appealed.

However, it amounts to a partial success in the BBC's attempt to force Trump to provide evidence for his claim of financial loss due to the Panorama edit.

The judge gave a split decision on a separate request by the BBC for documents relating to the Capitol riot itself.

The order came during the first hearing in the case, in which the US president is suing the corporation over an episode of Panorama that edited...

BreakingNewsTab.title: Tokyo urges men to wear shorts to work, but women say it's 'leg hair harassment'
BreakingNewsTab.text:
As temperatures rise this summer, Japanese men are baring more leg at work than ever before.

The Tokyo Metropolitan Government is pushing company employees to ditch their usual suit and tie for more casual attire, featuring t-shirts, trainers - and shorts.

Tokyo Governor Yuriko Koike introduced "Tokyo Cool Biz" in April, hoping to expand on her signature cool down initiative which has now become a widely recognised annual summer tradition.

Almost four months in, the newly allowed shorts in the office policy is getting mixed reviews.

Some commentators enjoy the relaxed rules, helping them work more comfortably. Others say the policy is unfair to women since they are still expected to wear tights when showing a part of their legs. And a few women describe the experience of "leg hair harassment" or a term coined online as "sunehara", referring to the discomfort of being forced to see their colleague's leg hair.

"We want to give people more options in the severe heat, not tell them what to wear. There should be no issue as long as the work attire is not offensive to anyone," Tokyo Metropolitan Government environmental official Noboru Watanabe told the BBC.

A June survey by Gorilla Clinic found 53.5% opposed wearing shorts to work in the summer while 46.5% were in favour of the new recommendation.

For those against shorts in the office the main reason, for both genders, was the concern over body shape and body hair. The clinic says women significantly outnumbered men in their response, indicating that women may be more resistant to seeing a male colleague's bare legs.

In recent years more Japanese workers, notably those working at startups and tech companies, have started wearing casual clothes. But this year, the Tokyo government recommendation has made it more socially acceptable. Still, some men have been feeling self-conscious about their leg hair.

Akifumi Funatsu, the director of Gorilla Clinic, says he has noticed more clients coming to the clinic not just for cosmetic reasons, but as a matter of "social etiquette when wearing shorts". He thinks women believe that "men should have less leg hair", which has prompted them to seek laser hair removal so as not to offend those around them.

Koike first introduced the summer wardrobe shift as Japan's environment minister in 2005. She launched a national energy-saving campaign called "Cool Biz" to encourage company workers to opt for short-sleeve shirts.

Government leaders participated in the initiative, with then Prime Minister Junichiro Koizumi often seen in public without wearing a tie or jacket, helping get the word out.

A few years later following the Tohoku earthquake and tsunami in March 2011, the government launched "Super Cool Biz" which expanded the guidelines to help reduce energy use both at work and at home.

As with other parts of the world, Japan has been experiencing record heat in recent months while grappling with soaring energy prices driven by instability in the Middle East.

Forecasters have warned of extreme heat this summer, with the Japan Meteorological Agency trying to raise public awareness by announcing the word "kokusho-bi", which means "brutally hot day" when the mercury exceeds 40C.

The latest weekly data shows seven people died in Japan from heatstroke and 4,580 people were admitted to hospitals in the seven days from 6-12 July, according to the Fire and Disaster Management Agency.

Besides staying hydrated and turning on the air conditioning, some of the most basic actions repeatedly encouraged by government officials, residents have turned to new products and gadgets to beat the heat.

Items such as clothes with built-in fans, disposable wet-towels, UV and heat blocking umbrellas, and portable handheld fans have been popular and sold in shops nationwide.

Experts from the Japan Academic Network for Disaster Reduction are stressing the need for heat acclimatisation as it takes several weeks for the body to adapt to high temperatures.

They say the key is to start preparing for hot weather and taking measures to prevent heatstroke during the country's rainy season, before the full summer heat arrives.

Japan also faces high humidity which makes it harder for sweat to evaporate, reducing the body's ability to cool itself.
BreakingNewsTab.description: As temperatures rise this summer, Japanese men are baring more leg at work than ever before. The Tokyo Metropolitan Government is pushing company employees to ditch their usual suit and tie for more casual attire, featuring t-shirts, trainers - and shorts. Tokyo Governor Yuriko Koike introduced "Tokyo Cool Biz" in...
BreakingNewsTab.author: SkunkNet Wire - bbc.co.uk

BreakingNewsTab.title: Sharon remembers Ozzy Osbourne one year on from his death
BreakingNewsTab.text:
Sharon Osbourne, the widow of heavy metal pioneer Ozzy, has used the first anniversary of his death to tell fans to remember him by playing music "really loud".

Osbourne spoke to the BBC exactly a year on from her husband's passing, which led to an outpouring of public grief, and celebration of his contribution to music.

In a rare interview about her feelings following the events of summer 2025, and her voice breaking with emotion at times, she told BBC Radio WM that she wanted people to "remember how funny he was - funny and charismatic and real".

"As Ozzy would say, 'when you want to remember me, just yank up the music really loud, as loud as you can get it, and just head bang'."

Osbourne was in her late husband's hometown of Birmingham as the city on Wednesday prepared to hold its first Ozzy Day, with a free celebration of the Black Sabbath frontman who was born and raised in the Aston area.

"He was authentic," she remembered.

"He never tried to be anything other than what he was, and he would tell you the truth about everything and that was him, you knew where you were with him.

"He was just genuine, so genuine and funny," she added.

"I mean we could have the worst arguments in the world but we would end up laughing because he would say something so ridiculous that we would just crack up."

One year on, Osbourne said she was also looking to the future, hoping that Ozzy Day would continue as part of the star's legacy.

"This is the home of metal," she said of Birmingham. "It's Ozzy's home, it's where he was born, what he loved and [Ozzy Day] wouldn't work anywhere else," she said.

"He loved this city, he loved its people. He was proud of being a Brummie and that's it."

In the meantime, there are the memories.

Osbourne described last year's Back to the Beginning gig, which included Metallica, Guns N' Roses and Slayer, as "beautiful".

"It was something my family, my grandchildren, my children will never forget," she said.

"The outpouring of love and respect that was there for Ozzy, and it just meant everything."

She added: "It went all over the world and people from all over the world were writing to us and saying what a beautiful thing it was."
BreakingNewsTab.description: Sharon Osbourne, the widow of heavy metal pioneer Ozzy, has used the first anniversary of his death to tell fans to remember him by playing music "really loud". Osbourne spoke to the BBC exactly a year on from her husband's passing, which led to an outpouring of public grief, and celebration of his contribution to...
BreakingNewsTab.author: SkunkNet Wire - bbc.co.uk

BreakingNewsTab.title: What can Hearts pick from debris of ongoing summer of hurt?
BreakingNewsTab.text:
There's crashing back to Earth with a bang, and there's whatever Hearts fans have been going through since 16 May.

As if losing out on their first league title in 60 years with moments left of the season wasn't bad enough, they have been unceremoniously stripped for parts by Rangers.

Captain, head coach, and talisman all gone, in that order.

Soon though, it would be a new dawn, a new day, and a new man to throw their weight behind as they continue to try and upset the apple cart of Scottish football.

Wouter Vrancken arrived as head coach, seemingly a coup from the continent.

But in his first game in charge on Tuesday, Hearts were battered 4-0 by Sturm Graz and any hopes of Champions League progression have died off quicker than you can say Christian Borchgrevink.

But is there cause for optimism in the underlying stats of the Belgian coach's first game in charge? After all, such data is Jamestown Analytics stock-in-trade...

It's been a summer of loss, grief and rebirth at Tynecastle.

It started barely a week after the 3-1 loss to Celtic that will - if those who can remember 1986 can tell you - haunt for years to come.

Hearts could barely have lost three more influential figures from the previous campaign, and each departure provided a blow that stung more than the last.

First it was captain and top scorer Lawrence Shankland. "At least we'll get a fee," some optimists might have thought. Nah, not even that.

A clause in his three-year contract, only signed the summer prior, allowed him to walk away for nothing.

Then, as Scotland were about to kick off their first World Cup campaign in almost three decades, there was the news that head coach Derek McInnes was also on his way to Ibrox.

OK, well, two boyhood Rangers fans. Perhaps it was to be expected.

But bad things come in threes, don't they?

Social media whispers suggested talisman and fan favourite Cammy Devlin was on the cusp of signing a new contract in Gorgie.

A searing statement on X from the club's official account soon ruled out a return, but 24 hours later, he was holding up a royal blue shirt and beaming in front of the camera.

If the days of Hearts lagging behind the Old Firm in the league are gone, the days of them being pillaged by the big two in Glasgow are not.

Before Devlin had even departed, there was a new head coach in charge. The process to appoint Vrancken was quick and initial signs were promising.

Here was a man who had been named Belgian coach of the year, had taken Racing Genk to within minutes of the title, and had been linked with Anderlecht and Burnley.

As well as a glowing CV, his previous style of play in Belgium was highlighted. It was not quite labelled all-out attack, but it was certainly going to be the style of football fans want to see.

Explaining his tactics in an early supporter forum, the man himself said he wanted "chaos within a structure".

In his first match in charge it was mostly chaos, not so much structure.

However, the 4-0 scoreline does not feel truly reflective of the game itself. The attacking stats showed Hearts did not lag behind their Austrian hosts by any means.

They created two chances in the first half that should have been buried, but Pierre Landry Kabore shot straight at the keeper and Alexandros Kyziridis nudged his effort wide when one-on-one.

By the time it came to creating any more, they were already 4-0 down. Inefficient in attack, torn apart at the back. It was a nightmare beginning.

"Early impressions are that Hearts have got a really good head coach," said Hearts Standard editor Joel Sked on a recent Scottish Football Podcast. "At the same time, just with everything that's happened, there will have to be some patience."

Patience is in short supply in football these days. For every Ange Postecolgou, there's a Wilfried Nancy. For every Jens Berthel Askou, a Russell Martin.

It is too soon to tell in which camp Vrancken will fall, but for the most part, Hearts fans remain cautiously optimistic.

They've been through it all in the past two months, nothing can surprise them. Not even this.
BreakingNewsTab.description: There's crashing back to Earth with a bang, and there's whatever Hearts fans have been going through since 16 May. As if losing out on their first league title in 60 years with moments left of the season wasn't bad enough, they have been unceremoniously stripped for parts by Rangers. Captain, head coach, and...
BreakingNewsTab.author: SkunkNet Wire - bbc.co.uk

BreakingNewsTab.title: Italy protests: Dozens hurt in Bologna after man dies while being restrained by police
BreakingNewsTab.text:
Protests have taken place in Italy after the death of a Moroccan-born man while being forcibly restrained by two police officers in the northern city of Bologna.

Several thousand people took to the streets on Monday night demanding "justice for Abderrahim Fakir".

A smaller group then splintered off and clashed with police into the night. Prosecutors say 64 police were hurt in the unrest.

Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni said it was imperative that the truth surrounding his death should be investigated but "nothing can justify violence against law enforcement".

Police had been called on Sunday morning after a neighbour witnessed Fakir "behaving erratically" and kicking garage doors in the working class neighbourhood of Pilastro. The officers tried to calm the situation but then used pepper spray.

A video circulating on Italian media shows two policemen sitting on Fakir as he lies face down and cries out for them to stop. A third person who is not a policeman pins his legs down as emergency workers stand by.

About two minutes into the video, Fakir stops struggling. The police officers tie his wrists and ankles and, when one of them realises Fakir is no longer moving, slaps him twice on the cheek. By the time an ambulance worker approaches, it is clear Fakir is unconscious.

Prosecutors in Bologna have opened an investigation into manslaughter against the police officers and the medical workers who were present at the scene.

Several eyewitnesses told Italian media that while Fakir appeared to be in distress he did not threaten anybody and was clearly unarmed.

"You can't die like this," his sister Khadija told Italian media. "They killed him mercilessly, and I want the truth."

Bologna mayor Matteo Lepore told Italy's La Repubblica newspaper that anyone who watched the video was "shocked".

He called for the demonstration in central Bologna on Monday evening and invited Fakir's niece to join him on stage.

"He was my uncle. He wasn't a number, he wasn't a news line... he was loved," Yossra Fakir said. "He deserved to be helped and heard, and he deserved dignity."

While the main demonstration on Monday night was peaceful, about 100 protesters clashed with police into the night and set two cars alight.

Lepore said on Tuesday he had seen "two different squares" in Bologna - "thousands of people gathered peacefully and spontaneously to express their support for Abderrahim Fakir's family" and a smaller group "who think they can respond to violence with violence".

Domenico Pianese, the Secretary-General of the police union, criticised Lepore's decision to call for a demonstration that later descended into chaos.

Calling for a protest "the day after a dramatic event like this one," Pianese said, was like calling "all the pseudo-anarchists and those who are anti-police... to take advantage and protest against the state".

Asked about what police got wrong when they detained Fakir, he said: "Judging from the images we saw, the intervention was carried out correctly."

Pianese argued that the fact the two officers had to use pepper spray and needed the help of a civilian to restrain Fakir showed that the use of force had not been excessive. "The first thing they did [after the incident] was... hand all [bodycam] footage to prosecutors," he said.
BreakingNewsTab.description: Protests have taken place in Italy after the death of a Moroccan-born man while being forcibly restrained by two police officers in the northern city of Bologna. Several thousand people took to the streets on Monday night demanding "justice for Abderrahim Fakir". A smaller group then splintered off and clashed with...

BreakingNewsTab.title: US launches fresh strikes on Iran, as Trump warns of retaliation for deaths of soldiers
BreakingNewsTab.text:
The US military says it launched another round of strikes against Iran to "further degrade" its capabilities to attack ships in the Strait of Hormuz.

The 10th consecutive night of strikes took place after US President Donald Trump vowed to punish Iran for the deaths of US soldiers.

In response, Iran said it struck two oil tankers in the waterway, as well as US military assets in Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan, state news agency IRNA reported.

President Masoud Pezeshkian said Iran was now fighting a "full-scale war" with the US. Trump earlier said Iran had been hit "very hard" the previous night "in honour" of three US soldiers killed recently - two in Jordan and one in Iraq.

US Central Command (Centcom) said its latest strikes had hit Iranian "military command centres, maritime capabilities, missile and drone launch sites, and air defence systems".

Explosions were reported near the north-western city of Tabriz, where state media said one person was killed and several wounded, as well at the Gulf ports of Bandar Mahshahr and Bandar Imam Khomeini and the south-eastern ports of Chabahar and Konarak.

Centcom also insisted that commercial vessels were continuing to pass through the Strait of Hormuz.

Iran meanwhile said its forces struck two oil tankers as they attempted to transit through what it called the strait's "unsafe and hazardous southern route" and rescue teams were picking up their crews, according to state media.

The United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations agency (UKMTO) said a tanker in the Strait of Hormuz had reported being struck by an unknown projectile, forcing its crew to abandon the vessel and board a lifeboat.

It was not clear whether the tanker reported by UKMTO was among those cited by Iran.

Six fuel tankers and cargo ships crossed the Strait of Hormuz on Monday, according to preliminary data from maritime intelligence firm Kpler. Two of these vessels had previously been sanctioned by the US for links with Iran.

This is down from eight vessels crossing on 19 July, the data shows. All but one of the crossings made on 20 July were via the northern route near Iran.

Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) also claimed to have targeted a data centre belonging to the US company Amazon in Bahrain, according to state media on Tuesday.

The IRGC "attacked and destroyed the central data infrastructure of the American company Amazon in Bahrain with several cruise missiles," state news agency IRNA reported.

Jordan said on Tuesday that it had intercepted five drones after saying it had shot down three missiles late on Monday. Bahrain said drones launched by Iran had targeted its air navigation systems - but traffic remained unaffected. Kuwait's armed forces also said they had intercepted Iranian drones.

Later on Tuesday Kuwait said Iran had hit several power and water desalination plants the previous day.

At the same time, Tehran said diplomatic messages were still being exchanged by both sides through intermediaries, despite a collapse of a ceasefire.

Speaking in the Oval Office on Tuesday, Trump insisted Iran wants to "desperately meet", but that "until they're ready to meet in a meaningful way, we have no interest".

Trump added that the US would "heavily" strike Mount Kolang Gaz La - also known as Pickaxe Mountain - following reports that Iran may have moved nuclear centrifuges to the area.

"It doesn't mean anything unless they have the [nuclear] material," he added.

On Monday night the Department of Defense confirmed the identities of two of the US soldiers – Pte Isabella Gonzales, 19, from Texas, and Lt Tyler James Feehan, 25, from Hawaii - who were killed in an Iranian strike on the Muwaffaq Salti base in Jordan over the weekend.

Centcom said earlier a third service member also went missing in action after the attack, and unidentified remains were later found in the area.
BreakingNewsTab.description: The US military says it launched another round of strikes against Iran to "further degrade" its capabilities to attack ships in the Strait of Hormuz. The 10th consecutive night of strikes took place after US President Donald Trump vowed to punish Iran for the deaths of US soldiers. In response, Iran said it struck...
