articles.title: Inside the horrifying online network where children extort others to commit violence and self-harm
articles.text:
In late 2024, in a quiet suburb of Stockholm, a 14-year-old boy approached a man in his 80s who was using a walker. He stabbed the man three times in the back.

The video of the attack in Hässelby was streamed and spread quickly. It fed a global online ecosystem that actively encourages and glorifies acts of violence and degradation – the “gamification of harm”, as the researcher Marc-André Argentino puts it.

The boy allegedly had links to a network known as 764. Such groups are known to target young people in games including Roblox and Minecraft as well as communities struggling with mental health or sexual identity for online extortion, pressuring them into acts of violence and self-harm.

The 764 network, founded by a Texas teenager, Bradley Cadenhead, is just one part of an evolving online ecosystem, with perpetrators and victims in dozens of countries. Cadenhead is now serving an 80-year sentence in the US for possessing child sexual abuse material.

There are several cases currently before courts around the world with alleged links to such groups. Legal records from numerous countries suggest they often celebrate violence, manipulation and harm for its own sake, collecting records of such acts to gain notoriety and control.

“These are young people recruiting other young people,” says Bjørn Ihler, the founder of the security firm Revontulet. “It is a youth phenomenon.”

The Australian federal police commissioner, Krissy Barrett, has said: “A worrying trend is how victims are becoming perpetrators to try to escape their online bullies who have befriended and groomed them, and then forced them to hurt themselves.”

A Swedish court decided in April 2025 that the teenager had carried out the stabbing although he was too young to be held criminally responsible. But another name emerged during the investigation, local authorities told the Guardian – an online username: “Chai.”

In early 2025, as the country was still reeling from the attack, an Australian girl rang Swedish police. She wanted to tell authorities about Chai.

‘It’s bigger than these few cases’

Linus Svensson, an investigative reporter at the Swedish broadcaster SVT, had never heard of 764 before the 2024 stabbing.

Svensson says he now knows of at least nine local court cases related to “the Com” – the term for a broad online network that engages in cybercrime, extortion and violence, and which includes 764. He says he has found dozens of Swedish members in chat groups.

“It’s bigger than these few cases,” he says. “I think it took a while for Swedish authorities and also the general public to actually understand this.”

As he began to wade through videos and imagery shared by the group for SVT’s investigation, Svensson came across a teenager in the north of Sweden who had been convicted in 2024 of trying to coerce a 13-year-old boy to take his own life.

This, according to Jenny Östling, a senior public prosecutor with Sweden’s National Unit against International and Organised Crime, was Chai.

The teenager, whose real name cannot be published, is on trial for more than 70 alleged offences, according to the indictment, including allegedly encouraging someone to self-harm and kill themselves, and the creation of child sexual abuse material. Most were allegedly committed against the Australian girl.

He is also charged with inducing a young man in Germany to harm himself so badly he almost died.

His lawyer, Emily Töyrä, told the Guardian he denies all the allegations.

The trial was paused in late June after the Swedish court sent him for a forensic psychiatric investigation, according to court records, stating there was “compelling evidence that [he] has committed several of the acts with which he has been charged”. The matter will return in August.

Östling says the case will test whether people can be held responsible for remotely coercing others into acts of self-harm or violence – a hallmark of groups such as 764.

“They have a goal for the group itself: to destroy the world … But [members] also have a goal for themselves: they want to gain status within the group,” Östling says.

Creating new material can be a prerequisite of entry to closed chatrooms and other markers of membership. “They primarily coerce young victims into cutting themselves or sexual acts,” she says. “They use it to be able to coerce them into doing something worse.”

Östling alleges Chai was “high status” within 764, and was invited into a chatroom known as “764 Inferno”.

His lawyer disputes that characterisation, and says her client’s involvement was limited to editing images and videos. “The Com environment is highly fluid,” Töyrä says. “My client does not accept the characterisation of ‘764 Inferno’ as the type of organised group alleged by the prosecution. In his view, it was simply one group chat among many.”

A global phenomenon

An indictment unveiled by the US Department of Justice in April 2025 alleges that two American men in their early 20s, Leonidas Varagiannis and Prasan Nepal, were part of a chatroom with the same name which “was reserved for the inner core of 764 members”.

The pair are accused of using “764 Inferno” to conspire with other members to create and distribute child exploitation images, among other charges. Researchers have found at least 295 arrests linked to the Com network in 33 countries, including Australia, where there are matters before the courts.

In New Zealand, after a tipoff from US authorities, Ronndog Keefe pleaded guilty in 2025 to 13 charges, including the distribution of “horrific and graphic” child exploitation material. The 22-year-old was sentenced to more than five years’ jail in December.

Keefe claimed he was also a victim of 764, which he said had threatened to dox him if he did not distribute such material, according to legal records, but the court found this “simply was not credible”.

Last year in the UK, Cameron Finnigan, then 19, was given a nine-year extended sentence for possessing a terrorist document – a “kill guide” with advice about how to commit “truck attacks” – and indecent images of children. He was also convicted of encouraging someone online to take their own life.

The court found Finnigan was part of 764. “The intended victims are … vulnerable individuals: in particular, LGBTQI+ youth, racial minorities and those suffering from mental illnesses or disorders,” the judge said at his sentencing. “They are targeted precisely because they are so obviously vulnerable.”

In June another British teenager, Elijah Palmer, was sentenced for encouraging serious self-harm, among other charges. He was also linked to 764, police said, and had targeted his victim on Roblox and the chat platform Discord.

The FBI uses the term “nihilistic violent extremism” to describe these activities but there is debate about the moniker. Some argue it is vague and tries to unite too many different groups under one umbrella.

Researchers such as Argentino have noted that ideology in these spaces can be “fluid, performative, and primarily symbolic” – occult or neo-Nazi imagery can function as markers of group membership rather than “as coherent political programs”.

Membership is also vague, according to Bjørn Ihler, who prefers to add the term “misanthropic”. “These are basically Discord servers,” he says. “There’s channels upon channels upon channels.”

Ultimately, the pursuit of sextortion material is about “power and … control of the victim”, Ihler says, and such content can be shared in so-called “lore books” for further victimisation by others.

“Sharing those materials … leads to increased ‘clout’, which is like the social currency,” he says. “Grappling with that is incredibly important from a victim-protection perspective.”

‘It could happen to anyone’

Long before these arrests took place, an independent researcher, Becca Spinks, had been tracking these groups from her home in Texas.

“As a mom, I’m just horrified,” she says. “I couldn’t close my eyes and just be like, ‘Oh, well, someone else will deal with it.’ Who’s working on this? What’s going on? How can we help?”

As part of her work, she started to speak to the mother of Chai’s Australian alleged victim several years ago, who was looking for help to get the attention of authorities and assistance for her child.

The trials of men linked to 764 taking place all over the world, Spinks says, have come as some relief. “She’s very brave,” she says of the alleged victim in Chai’s case. “And her mum also is really just a powerhouse.”

Chai’s trial has largely taken place behind closed doors but the toll the alleged crimes have taken on victims is clear.

Spinks says her biggest priority is raising awareness for parents and law enforcement officials.

“A lot of times kids come forward and maybe [the police] don’t recognise the signs that it’s the 764 network, and so they don’t pursue it as aggressively as they should,” she says.

Spinks says there needs to be greater awareness for parents too. The Australian federal police cite warning signs including indications of self-harm, sudden withdrawal from family and friends and secretive behaviour about online interactions.

“One of the biggest, most dangerous misunderstandings is that this won’t happen to us, or this only happens to a certain type of kid, when in reality, it could honestly happen to anyone.”

The video of the attack in Hässelby was streamed and spread quickly. It fed a global online ecosystem that actively encourages and glorifies acts of...

articles.title: Pokémon Go at 10 and the millions still trying to catch 'em all
articles.text:
Pokémon fans have spent the past three decades trying to catch 'em all - and since the beginning this hunt has often taken place outside the home.

The popular series started out on Nintendo's portable Game Boy console in 1996, and has found modern success on people's phones.

The mobile app Pokémon Go, which is now celebrating its 10th anniversary, uses GPS and augmented reality to let players find and catch virtual monsters in real-world locations.

It has been downloaded more than a billion times across iOS and Android devices, with millions still logging on each day.

And Michael Steranka, vice president of product at the game's publisher Scopely, says the Pokémon Go experience has always been about bringing people together.

"Pokémon Go will always start with community - we think we're only scratching the surface here," he says.

"We often receive wedding invites from players who met through Pokémon Go... because it's been such an integral part of their relationship."

It may seem a surprising sentiment to have for a game and its studio.

But Pokémon Go's connection with its players appears to have prevailed, as hundreds of gamers gathered in New York's Times Square on Thursday to battle a giant Mewtwo - a reference to the game's original trailer published over a decade ago, external.

When it was released in 2016, Pokémon Go quickly became one of the biggest mobile game launches in history.

The technology overlays digital creatures onto a live view of the real world through a smartphone's camera, making it appear as though they're standing in front of the player.

It sparked a craze where people flocked to places like parks, waterfronts and shopping malls in the hope of catching Pokémon.

"By allowing you to take your mobile phone out into the world to discover virtual creatures, Pokémon Go helped realise the millennial dream of becoming a Pokémon Trainer," said Matthew Reynolds, editor of Pokémon news website One More Catch, external.

The game's popularity has endured. It has hosted major live events in more than 60 countries, averaging more than 400,000 attendees a year since the first Go Fest in 2017.

Scopely estimates players have explored over 100 billion kilometres while playing Pokémon Go - roughly 334 round trips between the Earth and the Sun.

UK content creator j0beats runs one of Twitch's biggest channels dedicated to the game and regularly travels to events like the one in New York to meet fellow players.

"People always think it's crazy that you travel all over just to catch some pixels," she laughs.

"But it's not just about that. When you go to these big events, you're there for the people and for the energy of it.

"It's like a music festival - you could just stay at home and just listen to the music, or you could go out there and you could listen live and appreciate it with other people. It's the same sort of concept."

But the streamer says her fondest memory of the game is a little closer to home.

In 2025, Pokémon Go's Wild Area - one of its events - came to South Yorkshire.

"It was the only European event and it was hosted in Doncaster, which is sort of just around the corner from me," she said.

"So it was really exciting to collaborate with the community ambassadors there and help them host the event."

For some players, the game has also had an unexpected impact on their mental health.

Austin lives in Maine in the US and has been playing since 2017.

He told me before playing Pokémon Go it was "nearly impossible" to motivate himself to do anything, as he was feeling very anxious and depressed about life.

"When I went to my first raid meetup it was like a warm blanket," he said.

"As I held my phone and walked to the group of strangers in the park, I saw them look at me, and for the first time I wasn't nervous meeting this new group of people, I was actually excited and happy."

"From that day on that little voice telling me to stay in bed was put on mute."

Over the years, the game has sometimes been a victim of its own hype and global appeal.

Police and safety groups once warned players not to become so engrossed in catching the next Psyduck that they got lost or put themselves in danger.

The game's immense popularity sometimes also meant "servers buckled under the strain", said Reynolds, meaning connectivity problems were "rife for some time".

And while the pandemic was seen as a boon for many in the video game industry, Steranka says the initial strict lockdowns "impacted Pokémon Go probably more than any other game out there".

The game later bounced back as restrictions eased and people once again looked for reasons to get outside.

Then in 2025, some fans questioned how the game's future might change when Scopely - which is itself owned by Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund, bought developer Niantic for $3.5bn (then £2.7bn).

"My hope is that we prove to players over time that this is definitively a good thing for the game and the community," Steranka says.

Looking ahead, Steranka says the focus for the game remains on community, memories and creating experiences families can share.

"No matter where I was and what phase of my life, Pokémon Go has been there for me," he says.

"It meets people where they are, at whatever phase of life they're in."
articles.description: Pokémon fans have spent the past three decades trying to catch 'em all - and since the beginning this hunt has often taken place outside the home. The popular series started out on Nintendo's portable Game Boy console in 1996, and has found modern success on people's phones. The mobile app Pokémon Go, which is now...
articles.author: SkunkNet Wire - bbc.co.uk

articles.title: How a former Blue Peter presenter stunned America's Got Talent judges
articles.text:
A 50th birthday gift from her fellow Spice Girls, a picture of a dog and a mobile phone.

Those were the prized possessions that three America's Got Talent judges watched a former Blue Peter presenter appear to smash with a hammer.

But like all magic tricks, everything was not quite as it seemed and moments later the treasured items returned.

Joel Mawhinney, known as Joel M, said the "risky" trick was "the scariest experience" of his life.

But as he reminded the judges: "Everything that disappears must reappear."

Going into the judges dressing rooms and taking some of their prized possessions might not be the best way of getting them on your side.

Smashing those items with a hammer is even less likely to help your chances.

Yet that is exactly what Mawhinney did, leaving Mel B, Sofía Vergara and Howie Mandel stunned before revealing that every item had somehow survived.

Mandel's phone appeared inside a locked box that had been sitting in front of the judges throughout the audition.

The phone then began receiving texts from Mawhinney revealing where the other judges missing valuables had ended up.

Vergara's framed picture of her dog was hidden inside her seat cushion, while Mel B's bracelet was concealed in one of the famous Got Talent red buzzers.

"I don't know how you do it. I believe in magic but I loved it," Mel B said when giving her verdict, which was a yes.

Vergara laughed and said the "magic was very impressive". She also voted yes, adding: "I don't know if this was necessary, but it was very exciting."

Mendel called Mawhinney "charming, you've got a good look", but it was a no from the comedian, calling the performance "too slow".

Simon Cowell agreed on the length of the performance and said "you can't do that on a live show".

"Our audience love magic, which is exactly the reason I'm going to say yes."

With three yeses, Mawhinney progressed to the next stage and remains in with a chance of winning the cash prize.

Mawhinney said that when he entered the judges' dressing rooms he didn't know what he would have ended up with.

"I knew the plan was to take prized possessions, or whatever I could find, but it just so happened that the jewellery was there," he told BBC Good Morning Ulster programme.

"It's a hard and set rule that if I'm performing magic, I don't borrow possessions of people.

"I don't like taking phones. I don't like borrowing rings because I've just heard so many horror stories.

"But I thought, it's America's Got Talent, I have to do something. So I sort of went for it."

The former presenter said producers had been inviting him to audition for America's Got Talent since 2020, but he always felt it sounded like "a massive risk" and his "gut instinct was always no".

"But then I left Blue Peter this year and wanted to establish myself as a magician again, because that's what I'm doing now.

"And I thought, you might as well kick the whole thing off with something a bit extreme, so I'll fly to the States and do that."

Mawhinney, from Bangor in Northern Ireland, first appeared on Blue Peter in 2018 and later became the iconic children's show's 41st presenter.

After more than three and a half years on the show, he announced in July that he was leaving to focus on magic.

He said his experience presenting live television proved invaluable during the audition.

"The live training of Blue Peter meant I would've really struggled in that audition because there were so many different elements going on at once.

"I'm really glad I did it, but I mean, yeah, it's the most nervous I've been for quite a while because you're putting yourself into a massive risky position."

Those were the prized possessions that three America's Got Talent judges watched a former Blue Peter presenter appear to smash with a hammer. But like all magic tricks, everything was not quite as it seemed and moments later...

articles.title: BBC Introducing stage axed from Reading Festival
articles.text:
A stage at Reading Festival which previously hosted up-and-coming musicians including Ed Sheeran and Florence + the Machine has been axed.

Organisers Festival Republic announced an overhaul of stages last week, saying it would now be open to a "much wider pool of emerging talent".

As part of the plans, it said it would no longer host a BBC Introducing stage, despite the scheme having supported roughly 70% of all the UK artists on this year's line-up.

Tommy Longhurst, lead singer of Reading band Only the Poets, said the move was "sad news" for young musicians.

He said the music industry is already "really hard" for those who are just starting out, with grassroots venues struggling to stay open.

He said he believed the BBC Introducing stage "really does a lot for new artists".

Only the Poets have played several sets on the BBC Introducing stage at their home festival in previous years, most recently in 2025.

Longhurst said: "We hold those memories so highly and it was such a poignant moment for us to get to play a festival that inspired us to start the band.

"For me I think we're taking a huge opportunity from up and coming artists."

Organisers Festival Republic said its new Canopy stage would become the home for new and breaking acts.

It added: "Previously, only artists who had been selected by the BBC were qualified to play the Introducing Stage - now, we are able to open up this important platform to a much wider pool of emerging talent and book any new music artist for the stage.

"The change is fundamentally about supporting a more diverse range of up-and-coming artists, who will hopefully go on to headline the festival in years to come."

articles.title: Paramount and Warner Bros mega merger paused by judge
articles.text:
A US federal judge has temporarily blocked the proposed $110bn (£85bn) merger between media giants Paramount Skydance and Warner Bros Discovery.

The decision follows a lawsuit brought by a coalition of 12 US states, including California and New York, aiming to halt the deal over concerns it would stifle competition and raise consumer prices.

Prosecutors representing the states said merging two major studios would cause “substantial harm on movie theatres, basic cable distributors, and, ultimately, audiences nationwide”.

In response, the media giants argued that the states had misread the market and that merging would improve streaming efficiency.

US district judge Araceli Martínez-Olguín issued the temporary restraining order on Monday following legals arguments heard last week.

Under the 14-day injunction, neither company can finalise the deal or start joining the businesses together.

In her ruling, the judge noted that the state coalition raised "serious questions" regarding the deal’s impact on movie distribution.

She warned that allowing the merger to proceed now would make it "extraordinarily difficult to unscramble the egg" if the court decided later to block it all together.

Judge Martínez-Olguín also pushed back against the companies' arguments, highlighting that "public's vital interest in antitrust enforcement" outweighed any temporary delay to the merger.

She said that Paramount and Warner Bros "will continue to operate as separate, viable companies competing in the marketplace" while legal proceedings continue.
articles.description: A US federal judge has temporarily blocked the proposed $110bn (£85bn) merger between media giants Paramount Skydance and Warner Bros Discovery. The decision follows a lawsuit brought by a coalition of 12 US states, including California and New York, aiming to halt the deal over concerns it would stifle competition...

articles.title: £100,000 of TikTok sales in a day: The business of live shopping
articles.text:
Daisy Kelly's business was inspired by a personal problem - for years she'd been pulling out her eyelashes.

She hid her habit with lash extensions, but when beauty salons closed during Covid Daisy decided to create a serum to help her eyelashes grow back.

She started Glow For It from her mother's kitchen table in 2020 while she was a student and now her business generates £6m a year in sales.

More than 40% of Daisy's sales come from TikTok Shop UK and increasingly from livestreams.

"I think live shopping gives people that connection and interaction that we're all craving... We actually generated over £100,000 revenue in one 12 hour TikTok live," says Daisy, 27.

"It's categorically changed the trajectory of my business."

Daisy's business goes live from a studio for a minimum of six hours a day with different presenters interacting with shoppers, from showing off products to answering their questions.

"It's like having 500 people in your shop," she says.

There are a host of platforms and marketplaces now offering livestreams connecting sellers and customers, from Instagram Live, YouTube Shopping, to eBay and Amazon Live. They're all different.

Live shopping, it seems, is becoming part of everyday consumer behaviour for many.

According to new research from retail agency Savvy Marketing, 30% of shoppers surveyed said they'd bought something from a live shopping event.

"The big retailers have got to grab hold of this," says Catherine Shuttleworth, CEO of Savvy. "It's grown from nothing to a huge thing really quickly. If you haven't got a strategy for live selling, you're going to miss out."

TikTok Shop says its UK sales grew by more than 30% year-on-year in June, with live shopping the fastest-growing format.

It says it now has more than 6,000 livestreams taking place every day.

At an event in the centuries old Covent Garden market, it hosted 20 small businesses with stalls who were also selling live to customers all over the UK.

With nothing more than a smartphone, a lighting kit and a fridge full of fresh meat, halal butcher Manny Malik from Wolverhampton had around two hundred people tuning into his session.

"Last week we did a TikTok live for three hours and we sold £8,000 worth of meat," he says.

It would normally take him three to four days to sell this amount in his family shop, Malik Butchers.

They're now opening a second store in Stoke. TikTok, he says, is helping to drive his expansion.

But there are fees.

TikTok Shop UK keeps up to 9p from every £1 sold on the platform, as its standard commission.

As a comparison, eBay's marketplace business sellers typically pay between 6.9p and 14.9p depending on the category. Businesses selling on the dedicated live selling format, eBay Live, pay a combined fee of up to 8.42p including payment processing.

"It's a lot," says Manny.

"For us in the meat game, there aren't huge margins. However, it's opened up a new platform. We didn't have these sales prior to TikTok. We're not losing money, we've got enough profitability in there," he adds.

Buket Sevik, 34, founded her jewellery brand Adorina in February after quitting her 9-5 job as a business manager. Her new business went from 50 orders a month to 500 orders a week since she started live selling on TikTok UK shop in May.

She's gone from working from her dining table to live streaming from a small unit, where orders are now being packed.

"It's crazy," she laughs.

Demand is so high, she's trying to hire someone to help.

But for Buket, the best thing about livestreaming is the flexibility.

"I can go live whenever I want... which means it works absolutely perfectly for me as a mum. I can put my daughter to sleep and I can just hit go live and do some sales."

Savvy's latest survey of shoppers also found that 66% of Gen Y and Gen Z shoppers said seeing products sell out or go viral made them more likely to buy.

That's something that worries the free debt advice service, Money Wellness, which warns live shopping events are "a recipe for overspending", says external relations manager Rebecca Lamb.

"Every time you snap up a bargain before it sells out, your brain gets a dopamine hit - the same rush you get from gambling," she says.

"And because a lot offer one tap payment, there's no card, no basket and no total. Often you've spent money before your brain has caught up," she says.

She started Glow For It from her mother's kitchen table in...

articles.title: Edward Stourton: Broadcaster and journalist dies aged 68
articles.text:
Broadcaster and journalist Edward Stourton, whose career spanned more than 40 years including a decade on Radio 4's Today, has died aged 68.

Stourton, who had been diagnosed with prostate cancer in 2014, died on Saturday surrounded by his family, the BBC announced in a statement.

The presenter's friend and former Today co-host James Naughtie praised Stourton's "generosity of spirit", adding he was "a journalist of enormous capability and experience".

Another former Today presenter, Evan Davis, said Stourton was "a very important figure for all of us on the daily news programmes on Radio 4".

"One of those people you're really glad to have around," Davis reflected. "All substance, minimum ego, deeply knowledgeable, totally reliable.

"And he was one of the nicest of colleagues as well. He'll be missed."

Presenter Mishal Husain described Stourton as "a true gentleman and a broadcaster of immense capacity and intellect", while Justin Webb remembered him as "funny, self-deprecating and unpompous".

In his 2023 memoir Confessions, Stourton revealed he had been diagnosed with prostate cancer nearly a decade earlier.

He had treatment but was later told it had become incurable and spread. Stourton continued to work until very recently.

Stourton's career began at ITN, where he helped launch Channel 4 News and became the programme's first Washington correspondent in the latter years of the Reagan presidency.

He joined the BBC as Paris correspondent in 1988, and after a period back at ITN as diplomatic editor, returned to present the One O'Clock News on BBC One.

His BBC work also included Panorama, Assignment, Correspondent, as well as a phone-in programme on Radio 4, Call Ed Stourton.

Stourton became a presenter of the flagship Today programme in 1999, where his immense experience as a foreign correspondent served him well while reporting from countries such as Lebanon and Afghanistan.

He was also one of the first correspondents to reach New York after the 9/11 terror attacks.

His departure from Today in 2008 was leaked to a newspaper before he had been told, upsetting Stourton and many of his colleagues.

But he remained at the BBC and went on to present several other news programmes, while remaining as a contributor to Today.

In recent years, as well as writing and presenting documentaries for Radio 4, Stourton fronted the network's religious affairs programme, Sunday, as well as regularly hosting The World At One and The World This Weekend.

Stourton was born in 1957 in Nigeria, where his father was working, and also spent time as a boy in Geneva. He was educated at Ampleforth College and read English Literature at Trinity College, Cambridge, where he was President of the Union.

His Catholic faith was an important part of his life and Stourton fronted documentaries including Absolute Truth, a four-part BBC Two series about the Catholic Church.

It was also reflected in his writing - among Stourton's many books was John Paul II: Man of History, a revelatory biography of the former Pope.

His last book, written to coincide with the 250th anniversary of the American Declaration of Independence, was Made in America: the Dark History that Led to Donald Trump.

Paying tribute, Naughtie praised Stourton's "great integrity and humanity", adding that he "had a kind of infectious decency which lifted people up".

"He had a great effect on younger producers, who came away with enormous respect for the extent to which he tried to pass things on, and that he never patronised him,” Naughtie added.

"He was a man of wide cultural interests and experience and enthusiasm... His warm intelligence and wit were admired and enjoyed by generations with whom he worked.

"And as a friend, he was always a rock of support and kindness. His dignity and courage through his illness were an inspiration to everyone who knew him. He was, simply, much-loved."

The Today programme's current editor Rebecca Keating said she and colleagues referred to Stourton as the "Rolls Royce of broadcasting".

"His sharp mind and vast knowledge of the world brought to bear on any story with zero fuss," she wrote on X.

The BBC's interim news chief Jonathan Munro said it was "far too soon to lose Ed".

"I worked as his producer when he was ITN's diplomatic editor - unflappable then and ever after," he continued.

"Many years later, I was listening to his superb analysis of the new Pope for the BBC - another of his many areas of expertise. Not many broadcasters can present Today, BBC One's One O'clock News, The World at One and Sunday - but he did. He leaves a huge gap."

Today presenter Nick Robinson said Stourton "brought a ferocious intellect, boundless curiosity and a good deal of charm to all he did".

"He dealt with his cancer with resolution but without self pity," Robinson added. "He'll be badly missed."

Carolyn Quinn, who presented today alongside Stourton, remembered him as a "wonderful, erudite, generous colleague" who "was utterly dependable and unflappable as a broadcaster".

Radio 4 controller Mohit Bakaya described Stourton as an "absolutely outstanding broadcaster", adding that he "brought intelligence, authority and an extraordinary hinterland to everything he did".

ITV's Julie Etchingham, who also presented Radio 4's Sunday programme, said she was "very sad to hear the news" of Stourton's death, remembering his "huge intellect and great heart".

Stourton, who had homes in south London and south-west France, is survived by his wife Fiona, who is a senior television producer, two sons, a daughter and a step-daughter.

articles.title: Nottingham maternity scandal to be dramatised in new series
articles.text:
The largest maternity scandal in NHS history is set to become the focus of a new television drama starring Harry Potter actor Jason Isaacs.

Last month, a review found more than 500 mothers and babies suffered potentially avoidable harm or died due to failings at Nottingham University Hospitals (NUH) NHS Trust.

Jack and Sarah Hawkins - whose story will be told in the new TV series - campaigned for years for answers, after their daughter Harriet died before she was delivered at Nottingham City Hospital, which is run by the trust, in 2016.

NUH said the review was a "watershed moment" and it was "committed to creating a better maternity service" for women, babies and families in its care.

The programme will be based on the ITV documentary Maternity: Broken Trust, which followed families affected by the trust's failings.

Speaking to the BBC about learning the scandal would become a television drama, Sarah said: "I thought, that's crazy, it's so exciting.

"And I think the reason it's so exciting, is because for so long Nottingham families just felt so alone and that we were the issue.

"And I feel like now, we've come to the point where people are believing us."

The Hawkins were one of the first families to raise the alarm about maternity services at the trust.

When a hospital review found "no obvious fault", and stated Harriet had died of an infection, Jack and Sarah - who had both worked for the trust - pushed for an external review.

That external review, published in 2018, found 13 failings and concluded Harriet's death was "almost certainly preventable".

The wider review into maternity failings in Nottingham began in September 2022, and was led by senior midwife Donna Ockenden.

Sarah said: "The Ockenden review has demonstrated there there has been preventable harm and preventable death.

"And we did the amazing documentary before but I do feel like there is an element where people disconnect if it's a documentary because its just so horrific, people don't want to feel like there is preventable harm or death.

"With the drama, people will be able to get into it and there is another opportunity for people to see what Nottingham families have been through for so long."

Jack said he and Sarah knew there was a problem at the trust after Harriet died.

"We knew from the behaviour of the hospital in 2016 - and it's not the hospital, not the bricks and mortar - but the people," he said.

"And this is one of the interesting things about drama, you will get to see the behaviours of people that we were up against, who wanted us to pretend that it was one of those things, that it was normal, that it was reasonable that Harriet died, and that we were not surrounded by so many others.

"How can the avoidable death or harm to one child or mother be OK, and how can it be 500?

"We've normalised it, but this drama will show you the bones of what, actually, a fight against the NHS looks like, and the reality of what that fight was."

Jack will be played by Jason Isaacs in the drama, which is being produced by Cuba Pictures and directed by Jim Loach.

Laura Warner, who directed Maternity: Broken Trust - which won a Royal Television Society journalist award - is also an executive producer of the new series.

She said despite following the Hawkins for a year for the documentary, there was "only so much" they could fit into it.

"What you can't fit into a documentary is just the sheer depth of trauma and pain and frustration and struggle, that not just Jack and Sarah, but all the families in Nottingham have been through," she told the BBC.

"We barely scratched the surface with the documentary, as much as we tried hard, but I feel that this way, dramatising it, we can really try and put ourselves more into Jack and Sarah and the other families' shoes."

A transmission date for the series has not yet been announced.

Tracy Pilcher, chief nurse at NUH said: "Jack and Sarah Hawkins have been through unimaginable pain since the tragic loss of their daughter, Harriet, while in our care.

"Not only did we fail to care for them in our hospitals, but we also repeatedly failed them when they tried to get answers, and for that I am truly sorry.

"Through the pain and grief, they have campaigned relentlessly for answers and for change.

"The Independent Maternity Review into our services was a watershed moment for them and all other families involved in the review, getting the answers that they have fought so hard for.

"But the work doesn't stop there, there is still much to do to improve maternity care, and we are absolutely committed to listening, learning and creating a better maternity service for the women, babies and families in our care."
articles.description: The largest maternity scandal in NHS history is set to become the focus of a new television drama starring Harry Potter actor Jason Isaacs. Last month, a review found more than 500 mothers and babies suffered potentially avoidable harm or died due to failings at Nottingham University Hospitals (NUH) NHS Trust. Jack...

articles.title: Ofcom opens investigation into Married at First Sight UK
articles.text:
Married at First Sight UK is facing renewed pressure after the broadcast regulator launched an official investigation into the hit Channel 4 reality TV show.

In a statement on Monday, Ofcom said it was investigating "a fairness and privacy complaint" about the prime-time show.

In May, a BBC Panorama investigation revealed rape allegations from two female cast members on the show - allegations the men involved deny.

An Ofcom fairness or privacy complaint can be lodged by individuals who participated in TV or radio programmes. Complaints like these usually relate to claims of being treated unfairly or having unwarranted infringements of privacy on programmes.

It is understood the watchdog is considering a complaint of unjust or unfair treatment from a cast member of Married at First Sight UK.

At least one other cast member previously complained to Ofcom about their treatment on the show, but the watchdog dismissed their complaint as it found the broadcaster had no case to answer.

An Ofcom spokesperson said such complaints are considered "on a case-by-case basis through a detailed and careful assessment process".

Separately, in a letter to MPs in June, Ofcom said it would examine the findings of Channel 4's review into welfare on the show, and would "urgently" consider whether any further action is required.

At the time, the watchdog called the allegations exposed by Panorama "shocking and deeply disturbing".

Earlier this month, BBC News revealed that a cast member from the show had been arrested on suspicion of rape following the Panorama programme.

The individual is not being named; nor is the alleged victim. Anyone who makes an allegation of rape is entitled to anonymity by law.

Billed as a "bold social experiment", Married at First Sight UK - known to many as MAFS - sees single people agree to "marry" total strangers, after meeting for the first time at their mock weddings.

The UK version, which has been running for 10 seasons, is one of Channel 4's biggest shows, with audience figures often topping three million.

In May, Channel 4 pulled all episodes of the show from its streaming service just hours ahead of the transmission of the Panorama investigation.

No decision has yet been made on whether to broadcast the latest series of the show, which has already been filmed.

The broadcaster has commissioned a review into contributor welfare on the show, which is due to be finished later this summer. However, it only intends to publish "a summary of findings", rather than the full report.

Lawyers for CPL, the independent production company that makes the UK version of the show, have previously said its welfare system is "gold standard" and industry-leading.

If you have more information about this story, you can reach Noor by email at noor.nanji@bbc.co.uk
articles.description: Married at First Sight UK is facing renewed pressure after the broadcast regulator launched an official investigation into the hit Channel 4 reality TV show. In a statement on Monday, Ofcom said it was investigating "a fairness and privacy complaint" about the prime-time show. In May, a BBC Panorama investigation...

articles.title: Tributes paid to influential soul singer Ray King
articles.text:
Tributes have been paid to Ray King, one of the most influential singers in the ska and 2 Tone music genres, following the announcement of his death.

Born Vibert Cornwall in St Vincent and the Grenadines, he came to the UK in 1961 and arrived in Northampton, before moving to Coventry two years later, after being told it was the place to become a singer.

Pete Chambers, the founder of the Coventry Music Museum, said King had a stage presence "like James Brown".

"He was the kind of guy who controlled the stage with just one look, one little movement of the arm or whatever. He was a real professional and such a lovely guy and an influential guy."

In an interview with the Herbert Art Gallery and Museum, external in 2010, King said his musical influences as a young man and performer ranged from Sam Cooke, Nat King Cole, and Elvis Presley to Pat Boone and Doris Day.

He formed the Ray King Soul Band and performed throughout Coventry, with their first gig at The Walsgrave Club in the city.

The reputation of the band's professionalism and popularity led to a six-month residency at the Playboy Club in London, with the likes of Jimmy Hendrix and Paul McCartney joining the band on stage on an ad-hoc basis.

articles.title: ‘The Kardashian era is over’: TikTok’s ‘povvo king’ takes on the billionaires
articles.text:
It was ice cubes that tipped Shabaz Ali over the edge. “A woman online spent her entire day making content about ‘designer ice cubes’,” he recalls. “She’d freeze flavoured waters or fruit juices and show the whole process in tedious detail. I was like, ‘There’s no way she’s got a proper job or has ever struggled for money. Only someone wealthy would have the time to do this.’ It vexed me, so on a whim, I made a reaction video saying ‘On today’s episode of I’m Rich, You’re Poor …’ Soon my TikToks were getting 30m views and thousands of comments. It blew my mind.”

Ali, AKA @ShabazSays, has since become a cult celebrity for his sarcastic commentary on how the other half live. His scathing takedowns of the “rich and ridiculous” have won him more than 4 million followers. Now he’s transferring his viral fame to our TV screens.

It all began when former chemistry teacher Ali, 32, took to his bedroom in Blackburn during lockdown and amused himself by mocking the smugly privileged. In his crosshairs were those who humblebrag about their perfect lives and woo-woo habits. Tongue firmly in cheek, he styled himself as “King of the Povvos” and leader of the “#PovvoGang”.

“It was reclaiming an insult,” he says. “I grew up working class and heard ‘povvo’ so much. The community we’ve built is warm, honest and self-deprecating. They’re saying, ‘We don’t have much but at least we’re here’. Povvo is a badge of honour now.”

The disconnect between influencers and their audience is increasingly jarring. “They post about bespoke Bentleys and private jets. Who are you making this for? [US socialite] Becca Bloom really bothers me. Millions of people watch her cats eating out of £20,000 bowls. She has a £100k Louis Vuitton trunk full of Hermès bags. Yet her followers genuinely think they’re friends.”

In Ali’s corner of the internet, a backlash was brewing: “People were tagging me in dozens of videos per day, pointing out tone-deaf displays of wealth.” He calls it “a movement” that is helping to move the dial on unrealistic standards. “There’s been a massive shift in the last few years,” says Ali. “The Kardashian era is over. We don’t want airbrushed reality any more. We want it raw, authentic and unfiltered.”

In these days of doomscrolling, aspirational figures fill a void. “As a teacher, I saw how social media warped teenagers’ perception of the world. Wealth influencers, trad wives and Andrew Tate provide something that’s missing from their lives. The illusion of working hard and getting your reward has been shattered. They’re seeing their parents and teachers struggle. Young people dream of opulence but can’t have it. They’d say, ‘I don’t need to study, I’m going into business. Richard Branson didn’t do his GCSEs.’ I was like, ‘He’s one person!’ There are 3,400 billionaires in a global population of 8.3 billion. That’s a lot of disappointed people.”

Amid the cost-of-living crisis, his videos provided a reality check. “I was telling my students, ‘This lavish stuff online isn’t right. It’s not real’. That’s why I started taking the mickey. It snowballed.”

His BBC documentary, Shabaz Asks: Are Billionaires Bad?, finds Ali investigating extreme wealth and its real-world impact. With his trademark playful wit, he asks whether the mega-rich are a symptom of a broken culture or if they can be a force for good.

Wealth hoarding gives him “the ick”. “What annoys me most is that the gap is widening,” he says. “We’re getting more billionaires, who are finding more tax loopholes and ways to hoard assets. I genuinely think 90% of them don’t care. You can’t be a billionaire and be a good person.”

In a bid to understand more, he attempts to meet a billionaire in person. Ali approaches the Kardashians, Beckhams, Trumps, Elon Musk, Jeff Bezos and Beyoncé for an interview. “Kim K definitely saw my message. She didn’t reply, though. A couple of people hearted it. That was it.”

Instead he meets a range of people who are both pro and anti-billionaire. These include the Green party’s Zack Polanski. “He’s the only person on the party political spectrum who’s speaking out about billionaires,” says Ali. “Everybody else is too busy trying to protect their donors. Many of his arguments resonated. I’m a lifelong Labour voter but might I vote Green now? Possibly.”

He spends time with Apprentice winner turned “eight-figure entrepreneur” Joseph Valente (“fascinating character but he uses a lot of hairspray”). He flies to the Colorado ski resort of Vail to stay with Phones 4U founder John Caudwell. “I hate billionaires but I liked John, which was hard,” admits Ali. “I just think he’s unaware of his own power and privilege. He told me, ‘You can’t whisper in the ears of governments. I had a meeting with Keir Starmer and he didn’t take my advice.’ The fact that he even said that sentence! You got into that room because of money. The prime minister might not have taken your advice but there’s someone whose advice he did take. I admire John’s philanthropy but fundamentally we still disagree. Your rich friends are still wankers, mate!”

Ali takes the opportunity to poke around inside Caudwell’s kitchen, hoping to find one of the vast “curated” fridges he satirises online. “I genuinely thought I was going to get the internet lifestyle with everything neat and labelled but it was like a normal person’s fridge. It was dirtier than mine!” Did making the film challenge Ali’s views? “It didn’t alter my beliefs, it cemented them. I still think we don’t need billionaires and they should be doing more for the rest of us.”

He’s told that if we make billionaires pay more tax, they will leave the UK and take their wealth with him. “Let them go!” he says. “Let’s look over the hill and see if the grass is greener. It’s just scare tactics. They ain’t going anywhere. They’ve benefited too much from this country and have too much of a stake here to suddenly leave. Tax them, I say. Tax diamonds. Tax yachts.” He laughs. “If they don’t pay up, move them all to the Isle of Wight and turn it into a giant prison for rich people.”

This future star’s debut documentary ends with a shameless appeal for a full series. “We’ve already started talking about the next film,” he says. “Skincare, cosmetics and plastic surgery is the route I’m keen to go down. Shabaz asks: can I turn back time? That’s the question I want to pose. Watch this space.”

In the meantime, he believes society is approaching tipping point. “I think we’re on the brink of something big,” says Ali with a twinkle of zeal in his eyes. “People are so sick of wealth inequality. We’re French Revolution era, ready to overthrow Marie Antoinette.”

I was like, ‘There’s no way she’s got a proper job or has ever struggled...

articles.title: ‘His chords are like sheets of diamond ice’: the beguiling psych-folk of late cult songwriter Ed Askew
articles.text:
In a grainy television clip from 1986, a flame-haired and neckerchiefed troubadour plucks away at a lute-like instrument, harmonica under chin, the gentle ripples giving way to a voice full of longing. This performance, uploaded to YouTube in 2010, has generated only a handful of comments, but peruse any of them (“Ed the Great!”; “pure gold”) and you begin to understand the passion that Ed Askew – an under-the-radar US poet, artist and musician – has inspired over the years.

“He was living in a world that was for people who didn’t fit in,” Askew’s collaborator, Jerry David DeCicca, says on the free-spiritedness of his friend, whose musical life began with discovering the 10-stringed tiple as a teenager in the 1950s and ended with his death a year ago, aged 84, on the verge of him releasing his album The Final Painting, now being released posthumously this month.

Its producer, DeCicca, first fell for what he calls Askew’s “singularity” in the early 00s. But in recent years, he has captured the hearts of many more of music’s most characterful contemporary acts: from Sharon Van Etten and Bill Callahan, both of whom lend their vocals to The Final Painting, to Glasgow psych-folk band Trembling Bells who brought Askew to London’s Cafe Oto in 2017, and indie-Americana singer-songwriter Steve Gunn, who accompanied him at gigs in the early 2010s. “I got to tour with him in 2011 and felt his Jesus-y vibes,” Callahan says. “Jesus with a little scamp mixed in.” For Callahan, there is a “primordial” element in Askew’s music, that “makes it feel like the world is evolving right before your eyes when his song is playing. There’s a holy aspect to it – those piano chords constructed like sheets of diamond ice.”

From the scent of a marigold to the death of a Kennedy, what stands out the most about Askew’s songwriting is his flâneur-like observations, evoking the quotidian and the tremendous. In 2011, Askew cited his early influences as Kurt Weill, John Cage and Erik Satie, while others have tried to compare Askew to the likes of Robert Wyatt, Gaslight-era Bob Dylan or the Incredible String Band. The truth is that his baroque songwriting bears more resemblance to the multiple-angled paintings of Cézanne – an artist he greatly admired – or the writing of Joe Brainard mixed with the poetry of Lorca, than it does any of its musical equivalents.

Askew’s song Moon in the Mind appeared on the soundtrack of Netflix’s smash-hit series Baby Reindeer in 2024, an inclusion that was welcome and unexpected in the last year of his life, DeCicca says. But for the most part, Askew wasn’t concerned with acclaim or commerciality. “He just wasn’t wired that way,” DeCicca says; instead, Askew simply focused on writing his fine-spun songs on a day-to-day basis.

He displayed that beatnik essence from the very beginning. Born in Connecticut in 1940, Askew studied painting at Yale School of Art in the early 1960s, before moving to New York for a brief spell, playing for tips in the “basket house” folk music venues of the East Village in 1967. It was here that he attracted the attention of New York-based record company ESP-Disk – usually more inclined towards free jazz giants such as Sun Ra and Pharoah Sanders – who put out his 1968 self-titled debut (later retitled Ask the Unicorn), but soon shelved his follow-up Little Eyes, which wasn’t released until 2002.

Over the course of his life, Askew put out approximately 50 albums between labels, archival releases, live albums and self-releases, “with more still unreleased in his vault”, says DeCicca. This was mostly a home-recorded and self-published enterprise funded by house painting and art teaching, plus welfare assistance for a time – before he returned to the studio in 2011. Regarding a song on the new record titled Someone, with Callahan on vocals, DeCicca wonders if Askew is waiting in a social security office while they’re calling out numbers: “16”, “22” and “46” are delivered by Callahan with his usual deadpan delivery. “I think if most people wrote a song like that it would be about their agitation but Ed’s point of view is so different,” DeCicca says. But who knows if it’s really about social security at all, such is the impressionistic nature of Askew’s lyric writing.

Jay Pluck, Askew’s close friend and collaborator, points out just how pioneering Askew’s psych-folk songwriting was in the 1960s. “It was quite brave for him to be writing openly gay love songs so early on,” he tells me. And yet: “Over the years, I noticed that no one really knew that he was gay and I don’t know why – there are gay love songs on Ask the Unicorn and Little Eyes.” Pluck mentions a later song called Lamar, recorded on cassette tape in the late-90s, in which Askew is “professing his love in a Greek paean kind of way”.

Would Askew have described himself as a gay songwriter, I wonder? “I think he would,” Pluck answers – before pausing. “Although he could be a contrarian. If you asked him if he thought he was a great gay artist, he’d say no. But if you left off ‘gay’ he’d be like, why?” Pluck laughs. For Askew, his sexuality was simply who he was, as much a part of his songs as anything else.

Yet for Askew, “like most obscure musicians, there was never that Searching for Sugar Man blast,” DeCicca says, citing the 2012 documentary that launched Mexican-American musician Rodríguez out of obscurity and into late-in-life fame. “What allowed Ed to gradually find a global audience was not one event shining a spotlight; it was that he lived, day in and day out, as a true artist, sharing meaningful breadcrumbs for more and more people to discover.”

It’s a slow-burn tale in the age of the algorithm, and a fitting one for a musician who lived life on his own terms: “I play music for you to feel,” he once sang.

“There is no trying in his music,” Callahan says. “It just is.”

articles.title: The Odyssey: jaw-dropping action, dark magic and dolphin cameos – discuss with spoilers
articles.text:
The Odyssey looks set to be the blockbuster hit of the summer, with global opening weekend grosses of $264m, critical rave reviews including five stars from the Guardian’s Peter Bradshaw, and reports of fans traversing continents to experience Christopher Nolan’s epic tale of gods and monsters in one of the few dozen screens worldwide with the capability to screen Imax in 70mm. It also has the potential to rule the box office for the coming weeks, with few other true blockbusters to be released this summer, with the exception of Spider-Man: Brand New Day (hence the cast’s humiliation ritual of a press tour). Read on for a spoiler-packed breakdown of the epic battles, narrative twists and out-of-nowhere cameos that bring richness to Nolan’s idiosyncratic vision of ancient life.

A monumental spectacle

After Oppenheimer won best picture at the 2024 Oscars, Nolan knew he had a once-in-a-lifetime chance to realize a decades-long dream of bringing Homer to the big screen. “OK, I’ve now got an opportunity to make a film I wouldn’t be able to make otherwise,” he recently told the Guardian. You can see every penny of The Odyssey’s reported $250m budget on screen. The landscapes of Ithaca are breathtakingly beautiful, and there’s a creepy pall to our hero’s journey to the underworld that Nolan achieved by shooting on the black sands of Iceland’s beaches under the hazy light of the midnight sun. (Fun fact: Nolan also used Icelandic glaciers as the backdrop for the alien planet of Interstellar.)

Nolan filmed in six countries to achieve the range of landscapes in the film, which also included Scotland, Greece, Italy and Malta, and, more controversially, Western Sahara. Controlled by Morocco, the region is often referred to as Africa’s last colony, with the UN classifying it as a non-decolonized territory. “Audiences coming to see The Odyssey deserve better,” the artist and writer Mohamed Sleiman Labat wrote in an essay for the Guardian. “Those cinematic shots sold to them as the places and moments where historical epics took place were captured at the expense of the Sahrawi people’s suffering.”

The film’s epic scenes in the Mediterranean were filmed in the ocean, with Nolan eschewing CGI in favor of real roiling seas and Odysseus and his crew aboard the only functional Viking ship in the world (the delightfully named Draken Harald Hårfagre). A replica of the boat was built for scenes shot in a colossal water tank on the Universal lot. “There were these two jet engines, like 737 engines, blowing water on us,” Damon has said. “It was definitely about as hard as you can make water work in a controlled environment.”

Technical wizardry

Nolan has been a champion of Imax for two decades: in 2008, The Dark Knight was the first major Hollywood film to use the cameras for its action sequences. The camera set-up has been used increasingly prominently in his films since then. Nolan used half a dozen Imax cameras to shoot the film, all enclosed in soundproof “blimp” casing to mask their loud operational whirr.

The team also worked with an LED company to create lighting rigs they called “pyrohedrons” (fire pyramids) to give the illusion that warm firelight was casting a glow over interior and night-time scenes. “I wanted the camera to see what the human eye would see,” Nolan told the New York Times.

Bringing the divine down to earth

In Homer’s epic poem, gods are “self-interested beings, whose interventions in human lives are motivated primarily by their own desires, whims, and preferences”, as classicist Emily Wilson put it in her translation. Yet Nolan’s vision of how gods, demi-gods and mortals interact is somewhat earthier than in Homer, with Zendaya’s Athena less of a fearsome divine force than a pal and a confidante to Odysseus.

Charlize Theron is always a treat (even in her ropey Netflix fare), but Nolan’s interpretation of nymph Calypso may not feel strong enough for some viewers to work as a framing device for the bulk of the film. In Homer, the nymph is a cruel enslaver who holds Odysseus captive on her island; Nolan sees the character as lonely and somewhat pitiable. It’s worth wondering what Theron would have done with a spikier version of the role – she excels as a cartoon villain.

Like classicist Emily Hauser, who recently wrote for the Guardian about Nolan’s omissions, some viewers may also miss the Homeric princess Nausicaa. While interacting with Odysseus, she sniffily pooh-poohs him as “an unpromising man” – her barbs would have added a little light relief to the picture.

When Lupita Nyong’o was announced as Helen of Troy – the face that launched a thousand ships – rightwing commentators denounced the casting choice for predictably small-minded reasons. It is ironic that the character is afforded little screen time: when Tom Holland’s Telemachus goes to visit Helen and her husband, Menelaus (Jon Bernthal) in Sparta midway through the film, the famed beauty is near mute, with a grisly scar covering the left side of her face. It’s a bold choice from Nolan, but a little odd that we never find out the cause of her injury.

Odysseus’s loyal wife Penelope (Anne Hathaway) is a steely and cunning presence despite the suitors’ invasion of her palace. “We wanted to make sure that she did not look like she was mourning her husband,” said costume designer Ellen Mirojnick, who dressed Hathaway in the red and blue colors of Ithaca. “She was always viewed as the queen.” The actor has likened her character to a slowly simmering volcano that eventually erupts in scenes where she curses the suitors and pushes her double-crossing servant Melantho (Mia Goth) to certain death while the palace is at war.

One of the film’s most memorable scenes sees Odysseus and his men face the sirens, whose alluring song can lure sailors to their death. As their boat approaches their island, Odysseus is tethered to a mast to hear their beautiful yet terrible song while it is muffled to the audience’s ears, as if we, like Odysseus’s first mate Eurylochus (Himesh Patel), have our ears plugged with wax. The sound is “all the things you want it to be, then all the things you wish you never wished for”, says Odysseus, as Nolan powerfully leaves it to the audience’s imagination to fill in the blank.

Some of the film’s most touching moments come from John Leguizamo, barely recognisable with white hair and a dirty smock in the role of Eumaeus, the nearly-blind swineherd that remains loyal to Odysseus to the very end. As Greek military leader Agamemnon, Benny Safdie strikes a fearsome presence in a smart bit of against-type casting from Nolan. Meanwhile, not all cameos were planned: a pod of dolphins who made a surprise appearance on a day of shooting ended up making the film’s final cut.

Yet perhaps most surprising of all is the casting of rapper Travis Scott as a bard storyteller who opens the movie with a story of Odysseus’s heroic exploits at Troy. As he orates, the character pounds his staff on the floor in the tempo of dactylic hexameter (which as every good classicist knows, is the original meter of Homer’s poem). “I cast him because I wanted to nod towards the idea that this story has been handed down as oral poetry, which is analogous to rap,” Nolan said in an interview with Time.

It’s likely far too early to make many accurate predictions about next year’s Oscars, given that many of the awardsiest-seeming films won’t premiere until fall film festivals. But Nolan is one of the Academy’s favorite directors, winning seven out of Oppenheimer’s 13 nominations in 2024 and a respectable three out of eight for Dunkirk a few years prior. The Odyssey seems like a lock for nominations in best picture, best director and most of the craft categories, leaving the acting categories as a toss-up.

There’s no debate about who the male lead of the film is, but there’s all to play for in the supporting categories, with Holland and Robert Pattinson eligible for supporting actor and Zendaya, Nyong’o and Theron potential competitors in the supporting actress category – and Hathaway, if Universal decides not to run her in lead. But I’d personally lean towards Leguizamo for his turn as Eumaeus, and Samantha Morton’s staggering, scene-stealing role as Circe, a witch who turns Odysseus’s men into pigs.

Being something of an outsider to the Hollywood star system hasn’t stopped Morton getting two Oscar nominations to date (for Sweet and Lowdown and In America). With the weight of Nolan’s film behind her, I’ll be gunning for her to go all the way this time.

Examining the true cost of war

There’s a good case to be made that The Odyssey’s best sequence is its final 30 minutes, where Odysseus finally makes it back home with the help of Athena and sneaks into his Ithaca palace disguised as a beggar man. After Penelope challenges the men to string his bow – and Odysseus is the only one able to – he throws off his rags and reveals his true identity.

His face-off with the suitors isn’t the merciless slaughter that Homer described – it’s a fair fight with both sides armed – but the suitors, led by Pattinson’s slimy Antinous, are no match for the master of the house. His recapturing of Ithaca is bittersweet, interspersed with flashbacks to the “fires of anarchy and pain” that blazed through Troy as the Greeks sacked the city. There’s no glory in war for Nolan, echoing the sentiments of Dunkirk and Oppenheimer. As Odysseus puts it: “We have broken the fragile bond between men.”
